1.html:
Picture a single chart that answers the only question that matters before you risk a rupee. If NIFTY finishes higher up here, or flat in the middle, or far lower at the bottom, what do you actually make or lose? That one chart is the payoff diagram, and learning to read it is the real skill behind every named strategy in this course. A strategy is nothing more than a set of option legs stacked together, and its payoff diagram is the honest portrait of how that stack behaves. Master the picture and the fancy names stop mattering. You will look at any combination and say, in seconds, where it earns, where it bleeds, and how much is on the line. The OpenAlgo strategy builder draws this picture for you, and every chart in this course comes straight from it, built on real NIFTY option prices captured on 26 June 2026.

A payoff diagram is a picture of your money

Every payoff chart in this course shares the same two axes, so once you read one you can read them all.

  • The horizontal axis is the price of NIFTY at expiry. NIFTY is the index, the natural home of these strategies, because its options are the most liquid in the country. On 26 June 2026 the index sat at a spot of 24,056, with the at-the-money strike at 24,050. Every chart is drawn for the 28 July 2026 monthly expiry, the NSE monthly that falls on a Tuesday about 32 days out. As your eye travels left NIFTY has fallen, and as it travels right NIFTY has risen.
  • The vertical axis is your profit or loss on the whole position, in rupees for one lot of 65. Above the zero line you are in profit, below it you are in loss.

The single most important feature is the zero line, the level where profit and loss is exactly nothing. Wherever the strategy sits above it you are making money, and wherever it sits below it you are losing. The whole game is reading where the line lives at each possible closing price.

Key idea

A payoff diagram plots your profit or loss (vertical) against the price of NIFTY at expiry (horizontal), in rupees for one lot of 65. Above the zero line is profit, below it is loss. Read where the line sits and you know your outcome at any price.

The two lines: at expiry and today

Look closely at any builder chart and you will see two lines, not one. They tell different stories and you need both.

  • The orange "At Expiry" line is the value of your position on the final day, 28 July 2026, when all time value has drained away and only intrinsic value remains. This is the clean, kinked shape most people mean by payoff. It is what you keep or owe if you hold to the last bell.
  • The blue dashed "T+0" line is the value of your position today, right now, with zero days passed, which is what T+0 means. It is smoother and rounder because the options still carry time value.

The two differ because of time. An option is worth more before expiry than at expiry for the same price of NIFTY, since there is still time for the index to move your way. That extra worth is time value, and it melts away as expiry nears. By the final day the blue today line has collapsed onto the orange expiry line. So the blue dashed line is roughly what your screen shows now, and the orange line shows where you are heading if nothing is closed early.

Note

The orange At Expiry line is your destination. The blue dashed T+0 line is where you stand today. As the days pass, the blue line slides toward the orange one, because the time value baked into every option decays to zero by expiry.

Profit zones, loss zones, and the breakeven

Once you find the zero line, the chart splits into two shaded regions.

  • The profit zone is every closing price where the orange expiry line sits above zero. The builder shades this green.
  • The loss zone is every closing price where the orange line sits below zero, shaded red.

The exact price where the orange line crosses zero is a breakeven, the close at which you neither make nor lose. Some strategies have one breakeven, many have two, a few have four. The builder drops an amber breakeven dot on each crossing so your eye lands on it. Everything on one side of that dot is profit, everything on the other is loss, so the breakeven is the boundary fence between the green and the red.

Two flat landmarks finish this view. The maximum profit is the highest the orange line ever reaches, and the maximum loss is the lowest it ever sinks. For a defined-risk strategy both are flat, finite shelves, so you know your best and worst case before you ever place the order.

The sigma bands and the spot line

Two more features on every builder chart tell you what the market itself expects.

  • A bright pink vertical line marks the current spot at 24,056, so you can see at a glance how far each breakeven sits from where NIFTY trades today.
  • Behind the payoff sit faint shaded sigma bands, also called standard deviation bands. The inner pair marks a one standard deviation move and the outer pair a two standard deviation move, measured from the at-the-money implied volatility of about 12.7 percent and the days left to expiry. In plain words, they show the range the option market is pricing in. NIFTY is expected to finish inside the inner band about two times in three, and inside the outer band almost always.

Those bands are a reality check. If a strategy only profits when NIFTY lands far outside the outer band, the market is telling you that outcome is unlikely. If its profit zone comfortably covers the inner band, the odds are friendlier. You are reading the trade against the move the market actually expects, not against wishful thinking.

Tip

Before judging any strategy, glance at where its green profit zone sits against the faint sigma bands. A profit zone that covers the inner one standard deviation band is betting on a likely outcome. One that needs NIFTY beyond the outer band is betting on a rare one. The bands turn a guess into an informed read.

Legs: the pieces you stack

A leg is one single option in your position. You already know the four legs from Options Basics, the four atoms every strategy is built from.

  • Buy a call, the right to buy, for when you expect a rise.
  • Sell a call, the obligation to deliver if assigned, for when you expect NIFTY to stay below a level.
  • Buy a put, the right to sell, for when you expect a fall.
  • Sell a put, the obligation to buy if assigned, for when you expect NIFTY to stay above a level.

A strategy is simply two or more legs held at once, on the same underlying and expiry, chosen so their combined payoff has a shape you want. When you sell one leg, the premium you collect helps pay for a leg you buy. That is the quiet engine behind almost every spread. One leg funds another, lowering your cost or your risk in exchange for capping your reward. The combined payoff is just the sum of the legs, added up rupee by rupee at every price, and that adding-up is exactly what the builder does for you.

Did you know

The strategy builder draws the combined payoff of your legs from real NIFTY option prices, modelled with Black-76 off the synthetic future, the correct method for Indian options. The same engine produced every chart in this course. Spot 24,056, ATM strike 24,050, lot 65, strikes on a 50-point grid, expiry 28 July 2026.

Reading one real example end to end

Let us read a real strategy together, the bull call spread, so the whole picture clicks. It is a mildly bullish trade with two legs, both calls, on the 28 July 2026 expiry.

Leg Action Strike Type
1 Buy 24,050 Call
2 Sell 24,150 Call

You buy the 24,050 call and sell the 24,150 call. The call you sell brings in premium that pays down most of the cost of the call you buy, so the position costs far less than a lone call. That net cost, the net debit of Rs 3,624, is the most you can lose. Here is what the builder reports.

Number Value
Breakeven 24,106
Max profit Rs 2,876
Max loss Rs 3,624

Now read the chart. To the left, below 24,050, both calls expire worthless and you lose the whole net debit, Rs 3,624. That flat red floor is your maximum loss. As NIFTY climbs past 24,050 the bought call gains value and the loss shrinks, until at the breakeven of 24,106, marked by the amber dot, the orange line crosses zero into the green. Above 24,150 both calls are deep in the money, the spread is fully open, and your profit stops growing at its ceiling of Rs 2,876, a flat green roof. The blue dashed T+0 line shows the same journey rounded off, because time value is still present today and will be gone by 28 July. Notice that the breakeven of 24,106 sits just inside the inner sigma band, so this trade is betting on a modest, believable rise, not a moonshot.

That is the entire method. Find the zero line, the amber breakeven dots, the green and red zones, the two flat ceilings, the pink spot line, and the faint sigma bands. Do that on any chart and you have read the strategy. Every chart in OpenAlgo wears the same clean openalgo.in look, so the skill transfers to every payoff you will ever build. Before you risk real money you can also rehearse the order mechanics with sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), so nothing feels strange when real capital is involved. In the next chapter we slow down and study the full panel of nine numbers that sits beside every one of these charts.

2.html:
Almost every strategy in this course comes with a panel of numbers sitting right beside its chart. The OpenAlgo builder reports nine of them, and together they are the full scorecard of a trade. The chart shows you the shape; these nine numbers put exact figures on it. Learn to read all nine and you can size a position, judge its odds, and know precisely how much cash it ties up, all before you place a single order. This chapter teaches every one of them, using a real NIFTY trade you will meet again later, the bull put spread, and then sets it beside a very different trade so the most important lesson lands.

The nine numbers on the panel

Here is the full panel the builder shows for any strategy. We will define each, then read them off a real trade.

  • Max Profit: the best the position can do at expiry, per lot.
  • Max Loss: the worst it can do at expiry, per lot. "Unlimited" means it grows without bound, which only happens on the upside, from naked long or short calls and futures.
  • Breakevens: the price or prices where the orange expiry line crosses zero.
  • Probability of Profit (POP): the market-implied odds the trade ends in profit.
  • Risk to Reward: max profit set against max loss, shown as 1 to something.
  • Net Credit or Net Debit: whether you receive premium up front (credit) or pay it (debit).
  • Est. Premium: the gross premium of all the legs added together, a turnover figure.
  • Total P&L: the live profit or loss right now, about zero the moment you enter.
  • Margin Req.: the cash the exchange blocks to hold the position.
Key idea

Nine numbers describe a trade in full: max profit, max loss, breakevens, probability of profit, risk to reward, net credit or debit, estimated premium, live total P&L, and margin required. The chart is the picture; these nine are the exact figures behind it.

The bull put spread, in numbers

The bull put spread is a mildly bullish, defined-risk credit trade with two legs, both puts, on the 28 July 2026 expiry.

Leg Action Strike Type
1 Sell 24,050 Put
2 Buy 23,950 Put

You sell the 24,050 put, which pays you as long as NIFTY holds up, and buy the 23,950 put below it as protection. The sold put brings in more than the bought put costs, so you walk away with a net credit. Here is the full nine-number panel.

Metric Value
Breakeven 24,011
Max Profit Rs 2,506
Max Loss Rs 3,994
Probability of Profit 51 percent
Risk to Reward 1 : 0.63
Net Credit Rs 2,506
Est. Premium the gross premium of both puts combined
Total P&L about Rs 0 at entry
Margin Req. Rs 35,358

Walking the numbers one by one

Max profit, Rs 2,506. This is exactly the net credit you collected, and you keep all of it if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050, where both puts expire worthless. The credit is the reward, and you bank it just by NIFTY not falling.

Max loss, Rs 3,994. This is suffered if NIFTY closes at or below 23,950, where the bought put caps the damage. The two strikes are 100 points apart, worth Rs 6,500 for the lot of 65. You kept Rs 2,506 as credit, so the most you can lose is the rest, Rs 6,500 minus Rs 2,506, which is Rs 3,994. Credit plus max loss always equals the full strike width.

Breakeven, 24,011. NIFTY can drift down a little, all the way to 24,011, and you still come out even, because the credit cushions the first part of any fall. That is the 24,050 strike minus the credit of about 39 points a share. Above 24,011 you keep at least some profit, below it you start to lose.

Net credit, Rs 2,506. The position pays you on day one. A credit means premium lands in your account up front, the mark of a net seller. A debit would mean you paid up front, the mark of a net buyer. Here the credit and the max profit are the same figure, which is true of every simple credit spread.

Risk to reward, 1 to 0.63. For every rupee you risk you can make about 63 paise. You are risking Rs 3,994 to make Rs 2,506, so you risk more than you can make. That is not a flaw, it is the trade-off you accept in return for a higher chance of winning, which the next number explains.

Est. premium. This is the gross premium of both puts added together, a turnover figure, not your risk. Do not confuse it with the maximum loss. Your real risk is the Rs 3,994 max loss, never the gross premium.

Total P&L, about zero. The moment you enter, the position is worth roughly what you paid or received, so the live profit or loss is near zero. It moves into the green or the red as NIFTY travels and as time decays.

Margin required, Rs 35,358. This is the cash the exchange blocks to let you hold the trade, the SPAN plus exposure margin from the OpenAlgo margin calculator. Because this is a defined-risk spread with a bought protective put, the margin is modest. A naked short put with the same view would block several times more.

Did you know

For this bull put spread the strikes are 100 points apart, worth Rs 6,500 for the lot of 65. The credit of Rs 2,506 is your reward, and the remaining Rs 3,994 is your risk. Credit plus max loss always equals the full strike width. Every figure on the panel is per one lot.

Probability of profit, and the trade it implies

The number a beginner most often misreads is probability of profit, the POP. It is the market-implied chance the trade finishes in profit, worked out from a model of where NIFTY might land by 28 July, using the at-the-money implied volatility of about 12.7 percent and the days to expiry. You do not need the formula. Read it as the odds the trade ends a winner.

For the bull put spread the POP is 51 percent. NIFTY only has to hold near or above 24,011, slightly below today's 24,056, so the trade wins a little more than half the time. Pair that with the risk to reward of 1 to 0.63 and the full picture appears: a roughly even chance of winning, with a reward a little smaller than the risk. That is a balanced, honest trade, which is why it makes a good first credit spread.

Here is the rule that governs almost every strategy you will meet.

Note

A high probability of profit usually comes with a small reward, and a large reward usually comes with a low probability of profit. The market prices these so that no shape is free money. When a trade wins often, each win tends to be small; when a trade pays big, it tends to win rarely.

A high-POP trade: the short iron condor

To feel that trade-off, set the bull put spread beside a four-legged defined-risk structure you will meet later, the short iron condor. Ignore its construction for now and just read its numbers against the spread you already know.

Metric Bull Put Spread Short Iron Condor
Max Profit Rs 2,506 Rs 884
Max Loss Rs 3,994 Rs 5,616
Probability of Profit 51 percent 83 percent
Risk to Reward 1 : 0.63 1 : 0.16
Breakevens 24,011 23,864 / 24,236
Margin Req. Rs 35,358 Rs 68,864

The short iron condor wins 83 percent of the time, far more often than the bull put spread. That sounds wonderful until you read the rest of the panel. Each win is only Rs 884, while each loss can be Rs 5,616, a risk to reward of just 1 to 0.16. You are risking more than six rupees to make one. The high win rate and the small reward are two sides of the same coin: the market hands you the frequent wins only because it makes each one small and the rare loss large.

This is the single most important lesson in reading the panel. A high POP is not the same as a good trade, and a poor risk to reward is not the same as a bad one. The two numbers must always be read together, alongside your own honest view of where NIFTY is likely to go. Either trade can be sensible in the right conditions, and either can hurt you if you size it carelessly.

Tip

Never judge a strategy by its POP alone or its risk to reward alone. Read them as a pair. An 83 percent POP with a 1 to 0.16 reward and a 51 percent POP with a 1 to 0.63 reward are simply two different bargains, not a better one and a worse one. Your view on NIFTY decides which bargain fits.

Margin and defined risk, for beginners

Two of the nine numbers protect you more than the rest, the max loss and the margin required. Together they tell you what a trade can cost and what it ties up while it is open.

Both of these examples are defined-risk. The max loss is a flat, finite floor printed on the chart, Rs 3,994 for the bull put spread and Rs 5,616 for the short iron condor, because a bought leg caps the damage on each side. Their margins, Rs 35,358 and Rs 68,864, are modest for the same reason. Compare that to the naked short positions later in the course, where the loss is unlimited and the margin runs past a lakh and a half. As a beginner you want the trades whose worst case is a known number you can survive, and whose margin you can comfortably set aside.

Heads up

Always size a position by its max loss, never by its credit or its estimated premium. The bull put spread risks Rs 3,994 to make Rs 2,506, and a string of wins does not shrink the next loss. Decide your lot count by what your account can absorb on a bad day, keep every protective leg in place, and treat the margin figure as real cash that is locked away while the trade is open.

You now own the full scorecard. From here, every chapter introduces a strategy by its shape and its nine numbers, and you already know how to read all of them. Next we put them to work on the simplest trades of all, the single-leg long call and short put.

3.html:
You have finished Options Basics, so the four single legs are already familiar. Now we put them to work with a clear opinion. You think NIFTY is heading higher over the next month. There are two plain ways to back that view with a single option, and they sit on opposite sides of the very same trade. You can buy a call, paying a premium now for the right to ride a rise. Or you can sell a put, collecting a premium now and agreeing to buy NIFTY if it falls to your strike. One of you is the buyer, hopeful and patient with a small, fixed cost. The other is the seller, paid upfront but carrying a real obligation. This chapter teaches both on real NIFTY option data captured on 26 June 2026, with the index at 24,056, the at-the-money strike at 24,050, one lot of 65 units, and expiry on 28 July 2026.

The long call: pay a little, aim for a lot

The long call is the most natural bullish trade there is. You buy one at-the-money call and that single leg is the whole position.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 paid

The 24,050 call costs about Rs 27,677 for one lot of 65. That money leaves your account the moment you buy, and you can never be asked for a rupee more. That one fact is the whole comfort of buying options. Your maximum loss is the premium you paid, Rs 27,677, and not a paisa beyond it. In exchange, your upside is left wide open. Every point NIFTY climbs above the strike adds to the call's value with no ceiling, which is why the builder prints the maximum profit as Unlimited.

Here is the full nine-number panel for the long call.

Metric Value
Max profit Unlimited
Max loss Rs 27,677
Breakeven 24,476
Probability of profit about 31 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net debit Rs 27,677
Est. premium Rs 27,677
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 27,677

Notice the margin equals the debit. Because you cannot lose more than you paid, the exchange blocks only the premium itself and nothing extra. That is the cleanest margin in the entire course.

Key idea

A long call has a fixed, known cost that is also its maximum loss, here Rs 27,677, paired with an unlimited upside. You risk a defined amount to chase an open-ended gain. That shape, a small capped loss against a large open profit, is exactly why beginners are pointed at buying options before they ever sell them.

Reading the long call chart

Walk the orange at-expiry line from left to right. Far to the left, with NIFTY below 24,050, the right to buy at 24,050 is worthless, so the line rests on a flat floor at a loss of Rs 27,677, your maximum loss. As NIFTY pushes above 24,050 the call starts gaining intrinsic value and the loss shrinks. At the breakeven of 24,476, marked by the amber dot, the line crosses zero. NIFTY has to climb about 420 points, past the strike and far enough to earn back the whole premium, before you make a single rupee. Above 24,476 you are in the green and every further point is pure gain, the line rising without any ceiling.

The blue dashed line is the T+0 curve, the value today. It is smooth and rounded because the call still holds plenty of time value. As the days tick toward 28 July, that dashed line sinks toward the orange one, which is time decay working steadily against you as a buyer. The faint vertical bands are the sigma ranges, the spread of moves the option market itself is pricing in. The pink line marks spot at 24,056.

This is where the 31 percent probability of profit comes from. The breakeven of 24,476 sits above the plus one sigma band, so the market is implying only about a one in three chance NIFTY finishes there or higher by expiry. A long call is a low-odds, high-payoff bet, and the chart shows you exactly why.

Note

Time decay is the call buyer's quiet enemy. Even if NIFTY drifts sideways, the blue T+0 line falls toward the orange expiry line day after day, so the call bleeds value while you wait. A long call needs the move to actually happen, and reasonably soon, not merely eventually.

The short put: get paid to be patient

Now cross to the other side of the trade. Instead of paying for a call, you sell an at-the-money put and collect the premium upfront.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 received

You take in about Rs 19,182 the instant you sell, and that credit is the most you can ever make. You keep all of it if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050 on expiry, because the put you sold then expires worthless and the buyer walks away. You are, in effect, being paid to promise you will buy NIFTY at 24,050 if it sinks there. As long as it does not, you simply keep the rent.

The catch is the obligation. If NIFTY falls below 24,050, the put you sold moves against you rupee for rupee, exactly as though you owned NIFTY from 24,050 downward. The builder puts the maximum loss at a startling Rs 1,544,068, the cost of being forced to buy at 24,050 in a near total collapse toward zero. That is not a realistic single session outcome, but it is the honest theoretical floor, and it dwarfs the Rs 19,182 you collected.

Metric Value
Max profit Rs 19,182
Max loss Rs 1,544,068
Breakeven 23,755
Probability of profit about 62 percent
Risk to reward 1 : 0.01
Net credit Rs 19,182
Est. premium Rs 19,182
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 165,042
Heads up

The short put is a naked short leg. Its maximum profit is the Rs 19,182 you collect, but its maximum loss runs to about Rs 1,544,068 if NIFTY collapses, and the exchange blocks a heavy margin of about Rs 165,042 just to hold it. The risk to reward of 1 : 0.01 says it plainly. You are risking a large, lumpy amount to earn a small, fixed one. A beginner should study this trade carefully, not lead with it.

Read the chart. To the right, with NIFTY at or above 24,050, the orange line sits flat on its maximum profit of Rs 19,182. As NIFTY falls below the strike the line slopes down, crossing zero at the breakeven of 23,755, which is the strike less the credit you banked per unit. Below 23,755 you are in genuine loss, and the line keeps sliding toward the bottom left with no flat floor until NIFTY is near zero. The safe zone, everything at or above 23,755, is wide, which is why the probability of profit is a comfortable 62 percent.

Buyer against seller, both bullish

Both trades are bullish, yet they are mirror images, and laying them side by side teaches the central bargain of this whole course.

Long call Short put
You pay or receive pay Rs 27,677 receive Rs 19,182
Max loss Rs 27,677 Rs 1,544,068
Max profit Unlimited Rs 19,182
Breakeven 24,476 23,755
Prob. of profit about 31 percent about 62 percent
Margin Rs 27,677 Rs 165,042
  • The long call carries a small, fully defined risk and an unlimited reward, but it needs a real move higher to pay, so its odds are low at about 31 percent and it loses ground to time decay every quiet day.
  • The short put carries high odds, about 62 percent, and is paid by the passage of time rather than punished by it, but it caps your gain at the credit while leaving a large, lumpy downside and demanding heavy margin.

This is the buyer versus seller bargain in miniature. Buyers pay for the chance of a big, open-ended win and accept low odds. Sellers take high odds and friendly time decay in exchange for a capped gain and a tail of real danger. Neither side is right or wrong, but for a beginner the defined-risk long call, or better still the defined-risk spreads in the chapters just ahead, is the safer place to begin.

Tip

A simple way to choose. Buy the call when you expect a real, soon move and can accept losing the whole premium if it does not arrive. Sell the put only when you genuinely would be happy to own NIFTY near 23,755, can fund the heavy margin, and fully respect the downside. If you cannot say yes to all three, do not sell the put.

Did you know

On the same bullish view and the same 24,050 strike, the long call risks Rs 27,677 for an unlimited upside at about 31 percent odds, while the short put risks up to Rs 1,544,068 to earn Rs 19,182 at about 62 percent odds. High odds with a small reward, or low odds with a large one. Almost every strategy ahead is built by combining these two instincts.

You now own the two single-leg bullish trades and can read both straight off their nine numbers. In the next chapter we keep the bullish long call but tame both its cost and its odds by selling a higher call against it, building your first true spread, the bull call spread.

The short put: get paid to be patient

4.html:
The long call from the last chapter had a problem. To buy the 24,050 call you had to pay about Rs 27,677, and NIFTY then had to climb all the way to 24,476 before you made a single rupee, with only about a one in three chance of getting there. That is a lot to pay, and a long way to travel, for a view that might only be mildly bullish. If you expect NIFTY to drift up toward 24,150 rather than rocket away, there is a smarter, cheaper way to back exactly that opinion. You sell a higher call against the one you bought, and let the market fund most of your bet. This is the bull call spread, the first true two-legged strategy every options trader should learn, and a clean example of defined risk. The numbers here use real NIFTY option data captured on 26 June 2026, spot 24,056, lot 65, expiry 28 July 2026.

What a bull call spread is

A bull call spread has exactly two legs, both calls, on the same expiry.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 paid
2 Sell 24,150 Call about Rs 24,053 received

You buy the 24,050 call, the leg that profits as NIFTY rises. Then you sell the 24,150 call one strike higher, which brings premium straight back in. The cash you collect from the sold call pays down most of the cost of the call you bought. You started owing about Rs 27,677 for the lone call, but after the sold call refunds about Rs 24,053, your real outlay shrinks to a net debit of about Rs 3,624 for one lot.

Because you paid that money upfront and can never be asked for more, the net debit is also the most you can ever lose. That is what makes the bull call spread a defined-risk trade. You have traded away the dream of unlimited upside in return for paying a fraction of the cost.

Key idea

A bull call spread buys a lower-strike call and sells a higher-strike call on the same expiry. The sold call funds most of the bought call, so the position costs far less. That net debit, here Rs 3,624, is both your total cost and your maximum loss. You can never lose more than you put in.

The nine numbers

Here is the full panel the builder reports for this NIFTY bull call spread.

Metric Value
Max profit Rs 2,876
Max loss Rs 3,624
Breakeven 24,106
Probability of profit about 47 percent
Risk to reward 1 : 0.79
Net debit Rs 3,624
Est. premium Rs 51,730
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 35,378

Read the key three one at a time.

  • The maximum loss is Rs 3,624, the net debit you paid. You suffer it if NIFTY closes at or below 24,050, where both calls expire worthless and your whole outlay is gone. On the chart this is the flat floor on the left.
  • The breakeven is 24,106. NIFTY must close above this for the trade to make money. It sits a little above the bought strike of 24,050, because the index first has to rise enough to earn back the net debit before you cross into profit.
  • The maximum profit is Rs 2,876, reached once NIFTY closes at or above 24,150. Above that strike the sold call cancels any further gains from the bought call, so your profit stops climbing. On the chart this is the flat ceiling on the right.

Notice that the breakeven of 24,106 is far closer to spot than the lone call's 24,476, and the cost has dropped from Rs 27,677 to Rs 3,624. You profit on a much smaller move, which is the whole point.

Did you know

The gap between the strikes is 100 points, worth Rs 6,500 for a lot of 65. You paid Rs 3,624 of that as the net debit, so the most you can keep is the rest, Rs 2,876. The two numbers, max profit Rs 2,876 and max loss Rs 3,624, always add up to the full strike width of Rs 6,500. The spread simply splits that width between you and the market.

Reading the chart

Walk the orange at-expiry line across the chart. Below 24,050 both calls are worthless and you sit on your maximum loss of Rs 3,624, the flat floor. As NIFTY climbs past 24,050 the bought call gains intrinsic value, so the loss shrinks. At the breakeven of 24,106, marked by the amber dot, the line crosses zero and you move into profit. From 24,106 up to 24,150 you are in the green, your gains growing with every point. At 24,150 the sold call comes alive and starts offsetting any further gains, so the line flattens onto its ceiling of Rs 2,876 and stays there no matter how high NIFTY runs.

The blue dashed line is the value today, the T+0 curve, rounder because time value is still in both options. As the days pass toward 28 July, that dashed line settles down onto the orange one. The pink line marks spot at 24,056, and the faint vertical bands are the sigma ranges the option market is pricing. The breakeven of 24,106 sits just inside the plus one sigma band, which is why the probability of profit is a fairly even 47 percent, far healthier than the lone call's 31 percent.

Three ways expiry can play out

There are only three places NIFTY can finish relative to your two strikes on 28 July, and walking each one makes the chart shape obvious.

  • NIFTY closes at or below 24,050, say at 23,900. Both the 24,050 call and the 24,150 call expire worthless. The net debit is gone and you lose the full Rs 3,624, your maximum loss. This is the bet not working.
  • NIFTY closes between 24,050 and 24,150, say at 24,110. Your bought 24,050 call is worth 60 points of intrinsic value while the sold 24,150 call is still worthless. You collect that value, subtract the debit you paid, and the remainder is your profit. At 24,110 you are just past the 24,106 breakeven and barely in the green. This is the partial reward zone.
  • NIFTY closes at or above 24,150, say at 24,300. Now both calls have intrinsic value. The 24,050 call is worth 250 points and the sold 24,150 call costs you back 150, leaving a net 100 points, the full strike width, worth Rs 6,500. Subtract the Rs 3,624 debit and you bank the maximum profit of Rs 2,876. Anything above 24,150 gives the same result, because from there the sold call grows exactly as fast as the bought one.
Real example

If NIFTY closes at 24,130 at expiry, your 24,050 call is worth 80 points, about Rs 5,200 for the lot, and the sold 24,150 call expires worthless. Subtract the Rs 3,624 you paid and you keep roughly Rs 1,576. You are past breakeven but well short of the maximum, exactly as the rising middle of the chart shows.

When to prefer it over a long call

The bull call spread is not always better than a plain bought call. It is better for one common view and worse for another.

  • Choose the spread when you expect a moderate move, roughly to 24,150 and not far beyond. The sold call cuts your cost from about Rs 27,677 down to Rs 3,624, so you risk far less, your breakeven of 24,106 is much closer than the lone call's 24,476, and your odds rise from about 31 percent to about 47 percent. You profit sooner and on a smaller move.
  • Stay with the lone call when you expect a genuine surge, a violent run well past 24,150. The sold call caps your upside at the maximum profit of Rs 2,876, so a large rally that the lone call would ride all the way up is wasted on the spread. You traded that open-ended upside away in exchange for a much lower cost.

In short, the bull call spread says, I am bullish but realistic. You give up the dream of unlimited gains, and in return you pay less, risk less, and reach profit on a smaller move. For a beginner backing a mild view, that is usually the wiser trade.

Tip

Pick your sold strike at the price you genuinely expect NIFTY to reach, not higher in hope. The maximum profit sits at that strike, here 24,150. Selling a strike you do not expect NIFTY to touch just throws away premium and raises your cost for no benefit, while selling one too close caps your gain before the move is done.

What it costs and how to size it

Every benefit has a price, and for the bull call spread the price is your capped upside. You will never catch the full run of a runaway rally, because the sold 24,150 call gives back everything the bought call earns above that strike. You accepted that cap deliberately, in exchange for paying less and breaking even sooner.

On sizing, the comfort of defined risk is that the worst case is printed in advance. One lot risks Rs 3,624. Trade two lots and you risk Rs 7,248, and so on. Decide first how much you are willing to lose if NIFTY simply sits still or falls, then count back to how many lots that allows. Always size to the maximum loss, never to the maximum profit, because the loss is the number that actually tests your account.

One quirk worth noting. The margin the builder blocks here is about Rs 35,378, larger than the Rs 3,624 you actually risk. That is because the exchange margin system charges an exposure amount on the sold call leg even though the bought call fully caps it. The cash you can truly lose is still only the net debit, but be ready for the larger sum to be set aside while the position is open.

Heads up

Defined risk does not mean small risk. Rs 3,624 per lot is real money, and you lose all of it if NIFTY closes at or below 24,050 at expiry, which is entirely possible with the index sitting near 24,056 today. Size the position so that losing the full net debit, and having the larger margin tied up meanwhile, is something your account can absorb without strain.

You have now built and read your first real strategy end to end. The bull call spread is the cleanest expression of a mildly bullish, defined-risk view, and its logic carries straight into the rest of the course. In the next chapter we keep the bullish view but flip the construction, collecting a credit upfront instead of paying a debit, with the bull put spread.

toc.text: What a bull call spread is
Three ways expiry can play out
When to prefer it over a long call
What it costs and how to size it

5.html:
Here is a different way to be bullish. Instead of paying money upfront and hoping NIFTY climbs enough to pay you back, what if you got paid first, and simply kept the cash as long as the index did not fall? That is the appeal of a credit strategy. You collect premium on day one, and time works for you rather than against you. The bull put spread is the first credit strategy in this course, and it is the mirror image of the bull call spread you just built. Same bullish view, opposite construction. You receive money rather than spend it, and you win by NIFTY simply not falling. The numbers use real NIFTY option data captured on 26 June 2026, spot 24,056, lot 65, expiry 28 July 2026.

What a bull put spread is

A bull put spread has two legs, both puts, on the same expiry.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 received
2 Buy 23,950 Put about Rs 16,676 paid

You sell the 24,050 put, which brings in the larger premium, and you buy the 23,950 put one strike lower as protection. The sold put is the engine of the trade, collecting rent while NIFTY holds up. The bought put is your safety net, capping how much the position can lose if NIFTY does fall. The sold leg brings in more than the bought leg costs, so you keep the difference as a net credit of about Rs 2,506 the moment you open the trade.

That credit is the most you can ever make. You keep all of it if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050 on expiry, where both puts expire worthless. Because the bought 23,950 put caps the damage on the downside, your loss can never run away. This is a defined-risk credit trade, and that bought wing is exactly what separates it from the naked short put of chapter three.

Key idea

A bull put spread sells a higher-strike put and buys a lower-strike put on the same expiry. You collect a net credit, here Rs 2,506, which is your maximum profit. The bought put caps your loss, so unlike a naked short put your worst case is a known, finite number printed on the chart.

The nine numbers

Here is the full panel for this NIFTY bull put spread.

Metric Value
Max profit Rs 2,506
Max loss Rs 3,994
Breakeven 24,011
Probability of profit about 51 percent
Risk to reward 1 : 0.63
Net credit Rs 2,506
Est. premium Rs 35,857
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 35,358

Read the key three.

  • The maximum profit is Rs 2,506, the net credit you collected. You keep it in full if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050, where both puts expire worthless. On the chart this is the flat ceiling on the right.
  • The breakeven is 24,011. This is the sold strike of 24,050 less the credit you banked per unit. As long as NIFTY closes above 24,011 you finish in profit. Notice it sits below today's spot of 24,056, so NIFTY can even drift down a little and you still win.
  • The maximum loss is Rs 3,994, suffered if NIFTY closes at or below 23,950, where both puts are in the money and the spread is fully against you. On the chart this is the flat floor on the left.

The maximum profit of Rs 2,506 and maximum loss of Rs 3,994 once again add up to the full strike width, Rs 6,500 for the 100-point gap. The credit you took in is your slice, and the rest is what the market kept.

Did you know

The strike width is 100 points, worth Rs 6,500 for a lot of 65. You collected Rs 2,506 of that as credit, so the most the spread can take from you is the remaining Rs 3,994. Credit plus maximum loss always equals the full width. Knowing this, you can read a credit spread's worst case straight off its width and its credit, before you ever place it.

Reading the chart

Walk the orange at-expiry line from left to right. Below 23,950 both puts are deep in the money, the spread is fully against you, and you sit on your maximum loss of Rs 3,994, the flat floor. As NIFTY rises through 23,950 the bought put stops paying and your loss shrinks, until at the breakeven of 24,011, marked by the amber dot, the line crosses zero. From there up to 24,050 you are in the green, and at 24,050 and above both puts expire worthless, so the line flattens onto its ceiling of Rs 2,506. Anywhere at or above 24,050 you keep the entire credit.

The blue dashed line is the value today, the T+0 curve. For a credit trade it sits below the orange expiry line over the safe zone, which is the visual signal that time decay is on your side. Every quiet day, the options you sold lose a little value and that loss is your gain, so the dashed line rises toward the orange one as expiry nears. The pink line marks spot at 24,056, comfortably above the breakeven, which is why the probability of profit is a slight majority at about 51 percent.

Note

Time decay, the long call buyer's enemy in chapter three, is now your friend. As a net seller you are paid by the clock. The blue T+0 line sits below the orange expiry line across the safe zone, so the position quietly improves toward the full credit as the days pass, provided NIFTY holds above your breakeven. Sellers are paid to wait.

Three ways expiry can play out

There are three places NIFTY can finish relative to your two strikes on 28 July.

  • NIFTY closes at or above 24,050, say at 24,200. Both puts expire worthless. Nothing is owed, you keep the full credit, and you bank the maximum profit of Rs 2,506. This is the bet working, and it does not even need NIFTY to rise, only to hold.
  • NIFTY closes between 23,950 and 24,050, say at 24,000. The sold 24,050 put is worth 50 points against you while the bought 23,950 put is still worthless. That cost eats into your credit. At 24,000 you are just below the 24,011 breakeven, so you are in a small loss. This is the partial zone where the credit is only partly kept.
  • NIFTY closes at or below 23,950, say at 23,800. Both puts are in the money. The sold put costs you 250 points and the bought put refunds 150, leaving a net 100 points against you, the full width worth Rs 6,500. Subtract the Rs 2,506 credit you keep and you lose the maximum of Rs 3,994. Anything below 23,950 gives the same capped result, because the bought put matches the sold one rupee for rupee from there down.
Real example

If NIFTY closes at 24,030 at expiry, your sold 24,050 put is worth 20 points against you, about Rs 1,300 for the lot, and the bought 23,950 put expires worthless. You keep the Rs 2,506 credit and pay back Rs 1,300, netting roughly Rs 1,206. You are above the 24,011 breakeven and in modest profit, exactly as the chart shows just left of the ceiling.

Credit or debit, two roads to the same view

You now hold two bullish defined-risk spreads, and it is worth setting them side by side, because they answer the same opinion from opposite directions.

Bull call spread Bull put spread
Construction pay a debit collect a credit
Net cash debit Rs 3,624 credit Rs 2,506
Max profit Rs 2,876 Rs 2,506
Max loss Rs 3,624 Rs 3,994
Breakeven 24,106 24,011
Prob. of profit about 47 percent about 51 percent
  • The bull call spread needs NIFTY to actually rise above 24,106 to pay. It profits from a move up.
  • The bull put spread only needs NIFTY to hold above 24,011, which is below today's spot. It profits from a move up, a flat market, and even a small drift down. That wider safe zone is why its probability of profit is a touch higher, about 51 percent against 47 percent.

The trade-off is that the bull put spread's worst case, Rs 3,994, is a little larger than its best case, Rs 2,506, so you are risking slightly more than you can make. That is normal for high-odds credit trades. You accept a less flattering risk to reward in exchange for winning more often. Neither spread is better in the abstract. Pick the credit version when you mainly expect NIFTY not to fall, and the debit version when you expect it to genuinely climb.

Tip

A clean rule of thumb. If your view is NIFTY will hold up or rise, the bull put spread pays you to wait and wins on a flat tape. If your view is NIFTY will rise by a clear amount, the bull call spread rewards that move more directly. Let the shade of your opinion, hold versus climb, choose between them.

On sizing, treat the maximum loss of Rs 3,994 as the number that counts, not the credit. The margin the builder blocks is about Rs 35,358, larger than the Rs 3,994 you can lose, because the exchange charges exposure on the sold put even though the bought put caps it. Plan for that cash to be set aside while the trade is open, and never size to the credit you hope to keep.

You have now seen both a debit and a credit way to back a bullish view with capped risk. The next chapter steps up in ambition with three bullish shapes that trade those neat caps for bigger, more open-ended profiles, the call ratio back spread, the long synthetic future, and the range forward.

Three ways expiry can play out
Credit or debit, two roads to the same view

6.html:
So far every bullish trade has had a neat, finite shape. Now we step up in ambition with three structures that trade those tidy caps for something larger, more open-ended, and in two cases far more dangerous. They are all still bullish, but each expresses the view differently. The call ratio back spread keeps your downside small while leaving the upside wide open. The long synthetic future turns two options into a straight line that behaves exactly like buying a NIFTY future. The range forward is a bullish collar that mostly sleeps until NIFTY makes a real move. These are the trades where the strategy builder's nine numbers earn their keep, because the shapes are no longer obvious from the name. All figures use real NIFTY option data captured on 26 June 2026, spot 24,056, lot 65, expiry 28 July 2026.

The call ratio back spread

A back spread is built around an unequal number of legs. Here you sell one at-the-money call and buy two calls one strike higher.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 1 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 received
2 Buy 2 24,150 Call about Rs 48,107 paid (2 lots)

The sold call funds part of the two you buy, but the two cost more in total, so you pay a net debit of about Rs 20,430. In return you own one extra long call above the strike, and that spare call is what gives the structure its unlimited upside. The more NIFTY rallies, the more that net long call runs, with no ceiling, which is why the builder prints the maximum profit as Unlimited.

Metric Value
Max profit Unlimited
Max loss Rs 26,930
Breakeven 24,564
Probability of profit about 28 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net debit Rs 20,430
Est. premium Rs 75,784
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 52,256

The key surprise is where the loss lives. It is not on the downside. Below 24,050 every call expires worthless, so you simply lose the net debit. The real pain sits in the middle, at the 24,150 strike, where the sold call is fully in the money but your two bought calls have barely woken up. That is the maximum loss of Rs 26,930, the deepest point of the valley. Above it, the two long calls take over, climb through the breakeven of 24,564, and run without limit.

Read the chart. On the left, below 24,050, the line is a flat shelf at the net debit loss. As NIFTY rises into the 24,050 to 24,150 zone the sold call starts costing you faster than the bought calls pay, so the line sinks to its low point of Rs 26,930 right at 24,150. Past there the two long calls accelerate, the line turns sharply up, crosses zero at 24,564, and keeps climbing forever. The probability of profit is only about 28 percent, because NIFTY must clear 24,564, beyond the plus one sigma band, for the open-ended upside to pay.

Key idea

A call ratio back spread has a defined, finite maximum loss, here Rs 26,930 at the 24,150 strike, and an unlimited maximum profit on a strong rally. The danger sits in the middle, not the tails, so the worst outcome is NIFTY drifting up only mildly and pinning the sold strike. This is the rare structure where unlimited means unlimited profit, not unlimited risk.

The long synthetic future

The next structure abandons curves entirely. By combining one bought call and one sold put at the same strike, you create a straight diagonal line that rises and falls exactly like owning a NIFTY future.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 paid
2 Sell 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 received

The bought call gives you all the upside of a future, and the sold put hands you all of its downside. Together they cost a small net debit of about Rs 8,496, and the position then tracks NIFTY point for point in both directions. There is no flat floor anywhere, because there is no protective leg. This is the whole future, rebuilt from two options.

Metric Value
Max profit Unlimited
Max loss Rs 1,571,746
Breakeven 24,181
Probability of profit about 44 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net debit Rs 8,496
Est. premium Rs 46,858
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 173,537
Heads up

The long synthetic future behaves exactly like a long NIFTY future, which means its risk is two-sided and enormous. The maximum loss is about Rs 1,571,746 if NIFTY collapses, the sold put is a naked short leg, and the exchange blocks a heavy margin of about Rs 173,537. There is no defined floor here. This is a futures-grade position dressed as two options, and a beginner should treat it with futures-grade respect.

Read the line and there is nothing to interpret, which is the point. Above the breakeven of 24,181 you gain point for point with no ceiling. Below it you lose point for point with no floor, all the way down. The blue T+0 line lies almost exactly on the orange one, because a synthetic future holds almost no time value to decay. You hold this when you want pure directional exposure but prefer the options route, never because it feels safer than a future, because it is not.

The range forward

The third structure is gentler. A range forward, sometimes called a bullish collar, sells an out-of-the-money put below the market to fund an out-of-the-money call above it.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 23,950 Put about Rs 16,676 received
2 Buy 24,150 Call about Rs 24,053 paid

The sold put pays for most of the bought call, leaving a small net debit of about Rs 7,378. Between the two strikes, from 23,950 to 24,150, neither option has any intrinsic value, so the position simply rests on that small debit. It is a dead zone where nothing much happens. Outside the strikes it springs to life. Above 24,150 the bought call gives you unlimited upside. Below 23,950 the sold put hands you a future-like downside, deep and open.

Metric Value
Max profit Unlimited
Max loss Rs 1,564,128
Breakeven 24,264
Probability of profit about 40 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net debit Rs 7,378
Est. premium Rs 40,729
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 166,553
Heads up

The range forward erases your cost by selling a naked put, so the downside is future-like and very large, a maximum loss of about Rs 1,564,128, with margin near Rs 166,553. The flat dead zone between the strikes makes it feel calm, but that calm ends the instant NIFTY drops through 23,950, where the sold put begins losing rupee for rupee with no floor. Treat the downside exactly as you would a short put.

Read the chart and the range forward looks almost like the synthetic future, but with a short flat shelf in the middle where the two strikes straddle the dead zone. Above the breakeven of 24,264 you run with the long call. Below 23,950 you fall with the sold put. The structure suits a trader who is bullish and willing to be assigned NIFTY on a fall, in exchange for cheap, open-ended upside if the rally comes.

Three shapes, one bullish family

Strategy Max loss Max profit Breakeven Margin
Call ratio back spread Rs 26,930 Unlimited 24,564 Rs 52,256
Long synthetic future Rs 1,571,746 Unlimited 24,181 Rs 173,537
Range forward Rs 1,564,128 Unlimited 24,264 Rs 166,553

Read that maximum loss column carefully, because it sorts these three by danger. The call ratio back spread is the only one a beginner should reach for, since its worst case is a defined Rs 26,930 and it asks for modest margin. The long synthetic future and the range forward both carry a naked short leg, a near six-figure or larger downside, and heavy margin, because each is really a future in disguise.

Did you know

All three structures share an unlimited upside, but they differ entirely in what they risk to get it. The call ratio back spread risks a capped Rs 26,930. The synthetic future and the range forward each risk well over fifteen lakh on a collapse, because a sold put or sold combination leaves the downside open. Unlimited profit is attractive, but always check which side of the chart hides the unlimited, or near unlimited, loss.

You have now met the open-ended bullish shapes and learned to separate the one with defined risk from the two that behave like futures. In the next chapter we swing to the opposite extreme, the tightest and cheapest bullish bets of all, the bullish butterfly and the bullish condor, where the risk is tiny but the odds are slim.

7.html:
Most of the trades so far have been comfortable middle-ground bets, paying a few thousand rupees of risk for a few thousand of reward. This chapter goes to the opposite extreme. The bullish butterfly and the bullish condor are tiny, surgical bets that cost almost nothing and can pay many times their cost, but only if NIFTY lands in a narrow window at expiry. They are the cheapest structures in the whole course, with a maximum loss of barely a hundred rupees, yet they also carry the lowest odds, about one in ten. That combination, a cheap ticket on a long shot, is exactly what they are, and this chapter teaches them honestly as what they are good for and what they are not. All figures use real NIFTY option data captured on 26 June 2026, spot 24,056, lot 65, expiry 28 July 2026. Both structures are built entirely from calls.

The bullish butterfly

A butterfly has three strikes and four legs, arranged so that you buy the wings and sell the body twice. For a bullish butterfly the whole structure is nudged just above the money, so it pays best if NIFTY drifts up to a precise target.

Leg Action Strike Type
1 Buy 1 24,050 Call
2 Sell 2 24,150 Call
3 Buy 1 24,250 Call

The two sold 24,150 calls collect premium, and the two bought wings at 24,050 and 24,250 cap the risk on both sides. The premiums very nearly cancel, leaving a net debit of just Rs 120 for the whole lot. That tiny debit is also the entire maximum loss. You simply cannot lose more than Rs 120, no matter where NIFTY goes, because the wings box the structure in on both sides.

Metric Value
Max profit Rs 6,380
Max loss Rs 120
Breakevens 24,042 and 24,254
Probability of profit about 10 percent
Risk to reward 1 : 53
Net debit Rs 120
Est. premium Rs 96,333
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 63,628

The maximum profit is Rs 6,380, earned only if NIFTY closes right at the body strike of 24,150 on expiry. That is the sharp peak of the tent. The risk to reward is a spectacular 1 : 53, because you stake Rs 120 to make as much as Rs 6,380. But read the probability of profit before you fall in love. It is only about 10 percent, because the profitable window is narrow, running roughly from the lower breakeven at 24,042 to the upper breakeven at 24,254, and the full peak is reached at just one point.

Read the chart and the shape tells the whole story. Almost everywhere, far left and far right, the orange line lies flat at a loss of Rs 120, so close to zero it is hard to see. Only in the narrow zone around 24,150 does it spike up into a tall, thin peak. The two amber breakeven dots sit at 24,042 and 24,254, essentially at the outer wings, and the full Rs 6,380 is reached only if NIFTY pins 24,150 exactly. Step a little either way and the profit shrinks fast.

Key idea

A bullish butterfly is a cheap, precise bet that NIFTY drifts up to a specific target, here 24,150, and sits there at expiry. You risk only Rs 120 to make up to Rs 6,380, a risk to reward of 1 : 53. The catch is the probability of profit, about 10 percent. The shape pays brilliantly when you are right about the exact landing spot, and quietly costs you a tiny amount when you are not.

The bullish condor

A condor is a butterfly with its peak stretched into a plateau. Instead of selling the same strike twice, you sell two different middle strikes, which widens the top of the tent into a flat zone. That gives NIFTY a small range to land in rather than a single point, at the cost of a lower peak profit.

Leg Action Strike Type
1 Buy 1 24,050 Call
2 Sell 1 24,100 Call
3 Sell 1 24,200 Call
4 Buy 1 24,250 Call

Again the premiums nearly cancel, leaving a net debit of just Rs 88, which is the whole maximum loss. The two sold strikes, 24,100 and 24,200, form the edges of a flat profit plateau between them.

Metric Value
Max profit Rs 3,162
Max loss Rs 88
Breakevens 24,040 and 24,254
Probability of profit about 10 percent
Risk to reward 1 : 36
Net debit Rs 88
Est. premium Rs 96,366
Total P&L now about Rs 0
Margin required Rs 63,757

Read the chart against the butterfly's. The condor's top is no longer a single spike but a short flat shelf, sitting at the maximum profit of Rs 3,162 anywhere NIFTY finishes between about 24,100 and 24,200. The breakevens, at 24,040 and 24,254, are similar to the butterfly's, but the reward is lower, Rs 3,162 against Rs 6,380, because you traded peak height for a wider landing zone. The probability of profit is still only about 10 percent, since even the widened plateau is a narrow target relative to where NIFTY might travel in a month.

Note

The difference between a butterfly and a condor is the width of the bullseye. The butterfly pays the most but only at one exact strike. The condor pays less but across a small band of strikes. Choose the condor when you have a rough target zone rather than a single price, and the butterfly when your conviction about the precise landing point is strong.

The honest verdict on these long shots

These two structures are genuinely useful, but only if you understand what you are buying. The numbers tell the truth plainly.

Bullish butterfly Bullish condor
Net debit and max loss Rs 120 Rs 88
Max profit Rs 6,380 Rs 3,162
Risk to reward 1 : 53 1 : 36
Prob. of profit about 10 percent about 10 percent
Breakevens 24,042 and 24,254 24,040 and 24,254

The dazzling risk to reward, 1 : 53 or 1 : 36, is real but it is balanced by the slim 10 percent odds. About nine times in ten, NIFTY finishes outside the narrow window and you lose your tiny debit, a hundred rupees or so. About one time in ten it lands in the zone and you collect a multiple of that. This is not an income strategy and it is not a core position. It is a cheap, targeted lottery ticket on a specific landing spot, and it should be sized like one.

Heads up

Do not be seduced by the 1 : 53 risk to reward in isolation. The probability of profit is only about 10 percent, so the most likely single outcome, by far, is that NIFTY misses the window and you lose the small debit. These bets are cheap precisely because they rarely pay. Treat them as occasional, low-stake punts on a precise view, never as a reliable way to make money.

There is one more honest wrinkle. Although the cash you can lose is only Rs 88 to Rs 120, the margin the builder blocks is about Rs 63,000, because the structure contains sold legs that the exchange margins on exposure. So while your real risk is tiny, you still tie up meaningful capital to hold the trade, which makes these poor choices for a small or fully invested account even though the loss itself is trivial.

Tip

The right moment for a bullish butterfly or condor is when you have a clear target price and a clear expiry, for example you expect NIFTY to grind up to about 24,150 and stall by month end. Place the body at that target, keep the stake small because the odds are against you, and treat the rare big payoff as a bonus, not a plan. If you have no precise target, a defined-risk spread from the earlier chapters is the better bullish tool.

You have now seen the full bullish spectrum, from the simple long call, through the defined-risk spreads, the open-ended ratio and synthetic structures, and finally these cheap, surgical butterflies and condors. Each answers a different shade of the same bullish view, and you can now read any of them off its nine numbers. The next chapters turn the whole toolkit around to the bearish side, where every shape you have learned reappears as its mirror image.

toc.text: The honest verdict on these long shots

8.html:
You have spent seven chapters leaning bullish. Now turn the telescope around. Picture NIFTY sitting at 24,056 and imagine you think it looks tired, that the next month leans lower, perhaps back toward 23,500. There are two pure single-leg ways to act on that view, and they sit on opposite sides of the very same trade. You can buy a put, paying a premium for the right to profit as NIFTY falls. Or you can sell a call, collecting a premium and betting that NIFTY does not climb. One of these is the cleanest, safest bearish trade a beginner can place. The other is among the most dangerous positions in this whole course. This chapter teaches both, side by side, and is blunt about which is which.

The long put: paying for the right to a fall

A long put is the mirror image of the long call you met in chapter three. You buy the at-the-money 24,050 put expiring 28 July 2026, and you pay a premium for it. That premium gives you the right to sell NIFTY at 24,050 no matter how far it drops, so the contract grows more valuable the lower NIFTY goes.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 paid

The premium works out to roughly Rs 295 a share, or Rs 19,182 for one lot of 65. Because you paid that amount up front and can never be asked for a rupee more, the debit is also the maximum loss. You lose it in full only if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050 on expiry, where the put expires worthless. This is what makes the long put a defined-risk trade, and it is why the margin the exchange blocks is simply the premium itself, Rs 19,182, and nothing more.

Key idea

A long put buys the right to profit from a fall. You pay a premium, and that premium is the most you can lose. Your profit grows as NIFTY drops below the strike, so the trade rewards a real decline while capping your downside at the price of entry.

The breakeven is 23,755, which is the 24,050 strike minus the roughly 295 rupees a share you paid. NIFTY has to close below 23,755 for the put to put money in your pocket. The probability of profit is 38 percent, the market-implied odds that NIFTY finishes below that breakeven by 28 July. That figure is below half because you are paying for time value and asking the index to travel a fair distance down in about a month.

Reading the long put chart

The chart below is the real payoff, drawn from live NIFTY data captured on 26 June 2026, with spot 24,056, the 24,050 strike, lot 65, and about 32 days to expiry. The orange line is the value at expiry, the blue dashed line is the position today, the pink vertical marks spot at 24,056, and the amber dot sits on the breakeven.

Walk the orange line from right to left. Above 24,050 the put is worthless, so you sit on the flat loss of Rs 19,182, the red zone. As NIFTY slips below 24,050 the put starts gaining intrinsic value and your loss shrinks. At the breakeven of 23,755 the line crosses zero and you move into the green. From there it keeps climbing the further NIFTY falls. The metric panel reports the maximum profit as Rs 1,544,068, which is the theoretical gain if NIFTY collapsed all the way to zero. That will never happen, so treat that number as a label for "very large on a genuine decline" rather than a target. The honest takeaway is that a long put pays handsomely on a real fall and costs you only the premium if you are wrong.

Did you know

The reward to risk on the long put reads 1:80. That looks spectacular, but remember it pairs a tiny, certain risk against an enormous, very unlikely reward. The 38 percent probability of profit is the number that keeps you honest: most of the time NIFTY does not crash, and the put quietly decays.

The short call: collecting rent against a rise

Now the other side. Instead of buying a put to profit from a fall, you can sell a call to profit from the absence of a rise. You sell the 24,050 call and collect its premium the moment you open the trade.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 received

You take in about Rs 27,677 for the lot, and that credit is the most you can ever make. You keep all of it if NIFTY closes at or below 24,050, where the call you sold expires worthless. The breakeven is 24,476, the strike plus the roughly 426 rupees a share you collected, and the probability of profit is a comfortable 69 percent, because NIFTY simply has to avoid rising past that breakeven.

That high win rate is the seduction. Here is the catch that the credit hides.

Heads up

A short call has a maximum profit of Rs 27,677 and a maximum loss that is UNLIMITED. You have sold an uncovered call, so a sharp rally in NIFTY costs you rupee for rupee above the breakeven with no ceiling at all. The exchange knows this, which is why it blocks a margin of about Rs 1.78 lakh, nearly ten times the credit you collected. This is a naked short, and a single gap up can erase many winning trades in one morning. A beginner should understand it mainly to respect it.

Reading the short call chart

This is a long call flipped upside down. Below 24,050 you sit on your flat maximum profit of Rs 27,677, the green plateau. As NIFTY rises past the strike the sold call comes alive against you and your profit shrinks. At the breakeven of 24,476 the orange line crosses zero, and above it the line plunges into the red and keeps falling forever. There is no floor. The blue dashed today line sits below the orange expiry line on the profit side, because the time value you sold has yet to decay in your favour. Every calm day pulls the position toward that peak, which is the appeal, but the uncapped tail is always there.

The nine numbers side by side

Number Long Put Short Call
Max profit Rs 1,544,068 Rs 27,677
Max loss Rs 19,182 Unlimited
Breakeven 23,755 24,476
Probability of profit 38 percent 69 percent
Risk to reward 1:80 NA
Net credit or debit debit Rs 19,182 credit Rs 27,677
Margin required Rs 19,182 Rs 177,541
Total P&L at entry about zero about zero

Read the two columns against each other and the trade-off jumps out. The long put has a low probability of profit but a known, modest cost and a vast upside if NIFTY truly falls. The short call has a high probability of profit but a capped reward and a loss that can run away without limit, demanding a large margin to hold. One risks a little to chase a lot. The other collects a little while exposing a lot. That is the buyer versus seller bargain, drawn in its starkest form.

Note

The estimated premium for each of these single-leg trades equals its one premium, since there is only one leg: Rs 19,182 of turnover for the put and Rs 27,677 for the call. The total P&L sits at about zero the instant you enter either trade, because you have just paid or received fair value. Both numbers only start to move as NIFTY does.

Which one a beginner should reach for

If your honest view is that NIFTY falls, reach for the long put. Your worst case is printed on the ticket before you place the order, Rs 19,182 and not a rupee more, and the margin equals that same small figure. You can be wrong, lose the premium, and walk away with your account intact. That is exactly the kind of risk a learner should be taking.

The short call is a professional's tool, not a beginner's. The 69 percent win rate flatters it, but it pairs a small, capped reward against an unlimited, uncapped loss and a heavy margin block. The way experienced traders tame it is to buy a cheaper, higher call as protection, which converts the naked short into the defined-risk bear call spread, the subject of the very next chapter. That single bought wing turns "unlimited" into a known number.

Tip

When you want to be bearish and you are still learning, buy the put rather than sell the call. You give up the high probability of profit, but you gain a fixed, survivable worst case. The trader who is still in the game next year is almost always the one who refused to hold uncapped risk while learning.

You can rehearse both of these in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), placing each single leg and watching the orange line tilt one way or the other as you nudge the modelled price. Feel how the put gains slowly and the sold call gains quietly until the day it does not. In the next chapter we take that dangerous naked call and fence it in, building the bear call spread that lets you stay bearish while sleeping at night.

The short call: collecting rent against a rise
The nine numbers side by side
Which one a beginner should reach for

9.html:
The last chapter left you holding a problem. The short call is a tempting bearish trade, with a 69 percent chance of profit, but it carries an unlimited loss and demands a Rs 1.78 lakh margin block. You wanted to be bearish without betting the house. The bear call spread is the answer. It keeps the sold call that pays you when NIFTY stays calm or falls, and it adds a second, cheaper call bought a little higher up that caps the disaster at a known number. You give back a slice of the credit in exchange for a hard ceiling on your loss. For a beginner who wants to collect premium on a bearish view, this is the trade that the naked short should have been all along.

What a bear call spread is

A bear call spread has exactly two legs, both calls, on the same expiry. You sell the lower strike and buy the higher one.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 received
2 Buy 24,150 Call about Rs 24,053 paid

You sell the 24,050 call, the leg that pays you if NIFTY stays at or below the strike. Then you buy the 24,150 call a hundred points higher, which costs you premium but caps your risk. The call you sold brings in more than the call you bought costs, so the position opens with money in your pocket. That net is the net credit, and here it works out to about Rs 3,624 for one lot.

Because you collected that credit and the bought call limits how far the sold call can hurt you, this is a defined-risk trade. The most you can make is the credit you took in. The most you can lose is the gap between the strikes, 100 points worth Rs 6,500 for the lot, minus the Rs 3,624 you collected, which leaves Rs 2,876. Those two numbers always add up to the full strike width.

Key idea

A bear call spread sells a lower-strike call and buys a higher-strike call on the same expiry. You open it for a net credit, you keep that credit if NIFTY stays at or below the lower strike, and your loss is capped by the bought call. It is the defined-risk version of selling a call.

The nine numbers

Here is the full metric panel for the NIFTY bear call spread, built from live data captured on 26 June 2026, with spot 24,056 and expiry 28 July 2026.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 3,624
Max loss Rs 2,876
Breakeven 24,106
Probability of profit 53 percent
Risk to reward 1:1.26
Net credit Rs 3,624
Margin required Rs 34,789
Total P&L at entry about zero

Read them one at a time.

  • The maximum profit is Rs 3,624, the net credit. You keep all of it if NIFTY closes at or below 24,050 at expiry, where both calls expire worthless and the credit is yours. On the chart this is the flat ceiling on the left.
  • The maximum loss is Rs 2,876, suffered if NIFTY closes at or above 24,150, where the spread is worth its full 100-point width against you. This is the flat floor on the right, and it is the number that makes the trade safe to hold.
  • The breakeven is 24,106, the sold strike of 24,050 plus the roughly 56 rupees a share of credit you collected. NIFTY can drift up to 24,106 and you still do not lose money.
  • The probability of profit is 53 percent, a touch better than a coin flip, because NIFTY only has to avoid rising past 24,106. The reward to risk reads 1:1.26, meaning your capped reward is actually slightly larger than your capped risk, which is unusual and pleasant.
Did you know

Notice the margin. A naked short call blocked about Rs 1.78 lakh of your capital. This bear call spread, with the same bearish stance, blocks just Rs 34,789, because the bought 24,150 call defines the worst case for the exchange. The single protective wing cuts the margin to roughly a fifth and turns an unlimited loss into Rs 2,876. That is the whole point of the structure.

Reading the chart

Walk the orange at-expiry line from left to right. Below 24,050 both calls are worthless, and you sit on your maximum profit of Rs 3,624, the green ceiling. As NIFTY climbs past 24,050 the sold call starts costing you, so your profit shrinks. At the breakeven of 24,106, marked by the amber dot, the line crosses zero. From 24,106 up to 24,150 you are in the red loss zone, the loss deepening with every point. At 24,150 the bought call comes alive and stops the bleeding, so the line flattens onto its floor of Rs 2,876 and stays there no matter how high NIFTY runs.

The blue dashed line is the position today, the T+0 curve. It sits below the orange line on the profit side because the options still hold time value that you, as the net seller, have yet to collect. The pink vertical marks spot at 24,056, almost exactly at your sold strike, and the faint sigma bands show the one and two standard deviation move the option market is pricing into the next 32 days. As expiry nears, the blue line settles down onto the orange one and your credit ripens.

Three ways expiry can play out

There are only three places NIFTY can finish relative to your two strikes on 28 July 2026.

  • NIFTY closes at or below 24,050, say at 23,800. Both calls expire worthless. You keep the full Rs 3,624 credit. This is the bet working, and notice it works even if NIFTY merely sits still, not only if it falls.
  • NIFTY closes between 24,050 and 24,150, say at 24,100. The sold call is now worth 50 points against you while the bought call is still worthless. You give back part of the credit. At 24,100 you are just below the 24,106 breakeven, so you are roughly flat. This is the middle ground.
  • NIFTY closes at or above 24,150, say at 24,300. Both calls have intrinsic value. The sold 24,050 call costs you 250 points, the bought 24,150 call returns 150, leaving a net 100 points, the full width, worth Rs 6,500 against you. Subtract that from the Rs 3,624 credit and you are left with the maximum loss of Rs 2,876. Anything above 24,150 gives the same result.
Real example

If NIFTY closes at 24,100 at expiry, the sold 24,050 call is worth about Rs 3,250 against you for the lot, and the bought 24,150 call expires worthless. Subtract that from the Rs 3,624 you collected and you keep about Rs 374. You are barely on the right side of breakeven, exactly as the middle of the chart shows.

When to choose it

The bear call spread suits a specific, common view. Reach for it when you think NIFTY is more likely to drift sideways or fall than to rally, but you are not expecting a crash. You do not need NIFTY to drop at all. You only need it to stay below 24,106, which means time itself is on your side. As the days pass and the index goes nowhere, the sold time value decays into your profit.

It is the wrong trade when you expect a hard, fast fall. In that case the long put from the last chapter pays far more, because the spread caps your reward at the modest Rs 3,624 credit no matter how far NIFTY drops. The bear call spread says, "I think the upside is done, but I do not expect fireworks down." For that mild-to-neutral bearish view, collecting Rs 3,624 against a known Rs 2,876 risk, with time working for you, is a clean and beginner-friendly trade.

Tip

Pick the strikes around the level you genuinely think NIFTY will struggle to cross. Selling the 24,050 call places your breakeven at 24,106, just above the current spot, so you are paid for the index simply failing to rise. Selling a strike far above the money brings in less credit for the same width and wastes the edge.

Heads up

Defined risk is not the same as small risk. Rs 2,876 per lot is real money, and you lose all of it if NIFTY closes at or above 24,150, which a single strong up day can deliver. Always size to the maximum loss, never to the credit. Decide how much you can afford to lose if the index rallies against you, then count back to how many lots that allows.

You can build this spread leg by leg in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), watching the two flat shelves and the sloping middle form as you add the bought wing. Place the sold call alone first and see the loss line fall forever, then add the bought 24,150 call and watch that line snap flat at Rs 2,876. That single change, from a bottomless pit to a known floor, is the lesson of this chapter. Next we keep the bearish view but flip the construction, paying a debit instead of collecting a credit, with the bear put spread.
