Three ways expiry can play out

10.html:
There are two ways to build a defined-risk bearish spread, just as there were two ways to build a bullish one. The last chapter sold calls for a credit and won if NIFTY stayed put or fell. This chapter does the opposite construction. The bear put spread is built from puts, paid for as a debit, and it is the natural choice when you actively expect NIFTY to drop rather than merely fail to rise. It is the bearish twin of the bull call spread you met early in the course, flipped to the downside. You buy a put to profit from a fall, then sell a lower put to cut the cost, accepting a capped reward in exchange for a cheaper, defined ticket.

What a bear put spread is

A bear put spread has two legs, both puts, on the same expiry. You buy the higher strike and sell the lower one.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 paid
2 Sell 23,950 Put about Rs 16,676 received

You buy the 24,050 put, the leg that profits as NIFTY falls. Then you sell the 23,950 put a hundred points lower, which brings in premium and pays down most of the cost of the put you bought. A lone 24,050 put would have cost Rs 19,182. The sold put cuts that to a net debit of about Rs 2,506 for one lot. Because you paid that debit up front and can never owe more, it is also your maximum loss.

The most you can make is the gap between the strikes, 100 points worth Rs 6,500 for the lot, minus the Rs 2,506 you paid, which leaves a maximum profit of Rs 3,994. You reach it if NIFTY closes at or below 23,950, where both puts are fully in the money and the spread is worth its entire width. As always with a vertical spread, the maximum profit and the maximum loss add up to the full strike width.

Key idea

A bear put spread buys a higher-strike put and sells a lower-strike put on the same expiry. The sold put funds most of the bought put, so the trade costs less than a lone put. That net debit is both your cost and your maximum loss, and your reward is capped at the strike width minus the debit.

The nine numbers

Here is the full metric panel for the NIFTY bear put spread, built from live data captured on 26 June 2026, with spot 24,056 and expiry 28 July 2026.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 3,994
Max loss Rs 2,506
Breakeven 24,011
Probability of profit 49 percent
Risk to reward 1:1.59
Net debit Rs 2,506
Margin required Rs 34,113
Total P&L at entry about zero

Read them one at a time.

  • The maximum loss is Rs 2,506, the net debit. You lose it in full if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050, where both puts expire worthless. On the chart this is the flat floor on the right.
  • The maximum profit is Rs 3,994, reached if NIFTY closes at or below 23,950. Below that strike the sold put cancels any further gains from the bought put, so your profit stops climbing. This is the flat ceiling on the left.
  • The breakeven is 24,011, the bought strike of 24,050 minus the roughly 38 rupees a share you paid. NIFTY has to fall below 24,011 for the trade to make money, only a little under the current spot.
  • The probability of profit is 49 percent, almost a coin flip, and the reward to risk reads 1:1.59, so your capped reward of Rs 3,994 is comfortably larger than your capped risk of Rs 2,506. You are risking less than you stand to make.
Did you know

The breakeven sits at 24,011, just 45 points below the current spot of 24,056. That means NIFTY only has to slip a little for this trade to start working, unlike the lone long put whose breakeven of 23,755 demanded a 300-point fall. Selling the lower put pulled the breakeven much closer to the money, the quiet advantage of a spread over a single option.

Reading the chart

Walk the orange at-expiry line from right to left, since this is a bearish trade. Above 24,050 both puts are worthless and you sit on your maximum loss of Rs 2,506, the red floor. As NIFTY falls below 24,050 the bought put gains intrinsic value and your loss shrinks. At the breakeven of 24,011, marked by the amber dot, the line crosses zero into profit. From 24,011 down to 23,950 you are in the green, your gain growing with every point NIFTY drops. At 23,950 the sold put comes alive and offsets further gains, so the line flattens onto its ceiling of Rs 3,994 and stays there no matter how far NIFTY falls below.

The blue dashed line is the T+0 value today, smoother because the options still carry time value. The pink vertical marks spot at 24,056, sitting just above your breakeven, and the faint sigma bands show the move the option market expects over the next 32 days. As expiry approaches, the blue line settles onto the orange one.

Three ways expiry can play out

There are only three places NIFTY can finish relative to your two strikes on 28 July 2026.

  • NIFTY closes at or above 24,050, say at 24,200. Both puts expire worthless. Your whole Rs 2,506 debit is gone. This is the bet failing, and it fails if NIFTY simply holds or rises.
  • NIFTY closes between 23,950 and 24,050, say at 24,000. Your bought 24,050 put is worth 50 points of intrinsic value while the sold 23,950 put is still worthless. You collect that value, subtract the debit, and the result is a small profit just past the 24,011 breakeven. This is the middle ground.
  • NIFTY closes at or below 23,950, say at 23,800. Both puts have intrinsic value. The 24,050 put is worth 250 points, the sold 23,950 put costs you back 150, leaving a net 100 points, the full width, worth Rs 6,500. Subtract the Rs 2,506 debit and you bank the maximum profit of Rs 3,994. Anything below 23,950 gives the same result.
Real example

If NIFTY closes at 24,000 at expiry, your 24,050 put is worth about Rs 3,250 for the lot, and the sold 23,950 put expires worthless. Subtract the Rs 2,506 you paid and you keep about Rs 744. You are just past breakeven and well short of the maximum, exactly as the middle of the chart shows.

Debit or credit, which bearish spread to choose

You now know two defined-risk bearish trades that profit in the same direction. The bear call spread from the last chapter takes in a Rs 3,624 credit and wins if NIFTY stays at or below 24,050, time decay working for it. The bear put spread here pays a Rs 2,506 debit and wins if NIFTY actively falls below 24,011, time decay working gently against it. They reach for different shades of the same view.

  • Choose the bear put spread when you expect a genuine, fairly prompt decline. Its reward to risk of 1:1.59 is more generous than the bear call spread, and it pays you the most precisely when NIFTY drops to your lower strike. You want movement, and movement down is your friend.
  • Choose the bear call spread when you think the upside is capped but you are not confident NIFTY will actually fall. It pays you simply for the index failing to rise, and the clock is on your side. You want stillness, not a crash.

Against a lone long put, the bear put spread is the better trade for a moderate fall. It cuts your cost from Rs 19,182 to Rs 2,506, pulls your breakeven from 23,755 up to 24,011, and starts paying on a much smaller move. You surrender the put's vast upside on a collapse in exchange for a far cheaper, closer-to-the-money bet. For a beginner backing a measured bearish view, that is usually the wiser trade.

Tip

Set your sold strike at the level you genuinely expect NIFTY to reach on the downside, here 23,950. The maximum profit sits exactly at that strike. Selling a strike far below the money in hope of a crash collects little premium and leaves your debit high for almost no benefit.

Heads up

Defined risk still means real risk. Rs 2,506 per lot is gone in full if NIFTY closes at or above 24,050 at expiry, which it can do by simply holding steady from today's 24,056. Size to that maximum loss, not to the Rs 3,994 reward, and place only as many lots as your account can absorb if the fall never comes.

You can assemble this spread leg by leg in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), watching the cheaper, closer breakeven appear as you add the sold put to a lone long put. See how the deep profit line of the single put flattens into the Rs 3,994 ceiling, and how the cost drops from Rs 19,182 to Rs 2,506 in the same move. That trade-off, cheaper entry for capped reward, is the heart of every spread. Next we leave the simple two-leg spreads and explore three bolder bearish shapes: the put ratio back spread, the short synthetic future, and the risk reversal.

toc.text: What a bear put spread is
Three ways expiry can play out
Debit or credit, which bearish spread to choose

11.html:
The two bearish spreads you have just built were polite, defined-risk trades with capped rewards. This chapter steps up to three bolder bearish shapes that each chase a much larger payoff on a fall, and two of them carry uncapped risk to the upside that you must respect. You will meet the put ratio back spread, which costs little and pays enormously on a crash. Then the short synthetic future, where a call and a put combine into something that behaves exactly like selling a NIFTY future. And finally the risk reversal, a bearish collar that opens for a credit. Each is built from legs you already know, and each teaches a different way to lean hard on the downside.

The put ratio back spread

A ratio back spread deliberately breaks the one-for-one balance of a normal spread. Here you sell one at-the-money put and buy two puts a little lower, so you end up net long puts, which means you profit powerfully if NIFTY falls hard.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 received
2 Buy 23,950 Put (x2) about Rs 33,352 paid

You sell one 24,050 put for about Rs 19,182 and use that money to help buy two 23,950 puts for about Rs 33,352. The net result is a small net debit of about Rs 14,170 for the lot. Because you own one more put than you sold, a deep fall makes that extra put pay without limit on the downside, all the way to the theoretical maximum profit of Rs 1,536,080 if NIFTY collapsed toward zero. Treat that figure as a label for "very large on a crash."

The risk hides in the middle. Your worst case is not on the upside but at the lower strike. If NIFTY closes exactly at 23,950, the put you sold is 100 points in the money against you while both puts you bought expire worthless, and you also lose the debit. That maximum loss is Rs 20,670, the deepest point of a small valley. Above 24,050 all three puts expire worthless and you simply lose the Rs 14,170 debit. The breakeven is 23,632, well below the valley, where the second long put has finally earned back everything.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 1,536,080
Max loss Rs 20,670
Breakeven 23,632
Probability of profit 32 percent
Risk to reward 1:74
Net debit Rs 14,170
Margin required Rs 46,304
Key idea

A put ratio back spread sells one put and buys two lower puts, leaving you net long a put. It costs a small debit, takes its worst loss if NIFTY drifts only mildly down to the short strike, and pays without limit on a genuine collapse. It is a cheap, high-conviction bet on a hard fall, not a mild one.

The 32 percent probability of profit is the honest catch. This trade rewards a big, fast decline. A small slide to 23,950 is actually the worst place NIFTY can land, which feels backwards until you see the valley on the chart. Reach for it only when you expect a real break lower, not a gentle drift.

The short synthetic future

The next shape copies a futures position out of two options. Sell the at-the-money call and buy the at-the-money put at the same 24,050 strike, and the pair behaves exactly like a short NIFTY future: a single straight diagonal line of profit and loss.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 received
2 Buy 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 paid

The call you sell brings in more than the put you buy costs, so the position opens for a small net credit of about Rs 8,496. From there it is a straight line. Every point NIFTY falls, you gain 65 rupees. Every point it rises, you lose 65 rupees. The breakeven is 24,181, the strike plus the credit per share, and that level is the synthetic future price, sitting just above spot because of the cost of carry baked into the options.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 1,571,746
Max loss Unlimited
Breakeven 24,181
Probability of profit 56 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net credit Rs 8,496
Margin required Rs 169,046
Heads up

A short synthetic future has a maximum loss that is UNLIMITED. Because you sold a call with no protection above it, the position loses 65 rupees for every point NIFTY rises, with no ceiling. It is a leveraged short on the index, which is why the exchange blocks a margin of about Rs 1.69 lakh. The 56 percent probability of profit does not soften the uncapped tail. A sharp rally can run the loss far past anything you collected.

The synthetic future is not a beginner's first trade. Its value is conceptual: it shows that a call and a put are two halves of the same instrument, and that selling a call while buying a put equals being short the future. Understanding that identity makes every later structure easier to read.

The risk reversal

The last shape softens the synthetic into a bearish collar. You buy a put below the money for protection and sell a call above the money to pay for it, leaving a gentle slope in between.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 23,950 Put about Rs 16,676 paid
2 Sell 24,150 Call about Rs 24,053 received

The call you sell brings in more than the put you buy costs, so the risk reversal opens for a net credit of about Rs 7,378. Below 23,950 the long put profits and the gain grows the further NIFTY falls, toward a very large maximum if it collapses. Between the strikes, from 23,950 to 24,150, you simply hold the credit, give or take. Above 24,150 the sold call turns against you and the loss runs without limit. The breakeven is 24,264, the call strike plus the credit per share.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 1,564,128
Max loss Unlimited
Breakeven 24,264
Probability of profit 60 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net credit Rs 7,378
Margin required Rs 163,941
Heads up

A bearish risk reversal has a maximum loss that is UNLIMITED. The sold 24,150 call has no protection above it, so a rally past 24,264 costs you without limit and the exchange blocks about Rs 1.64 lakh of margin. The 60 percent probability of profit comes entirely from the wide gap before the loss begins, but that gap does not cap the tail. Treat it as a leveraged bearish position, not a safe income trade.

How the three compare

Strategy Net Max loss Probability of profit Breakeven
Put ratio back spread debit Rs 14,170 Rs 20,670 32 percent 23,632
Short synthetic future credit Rs 8,496 Unlimited 56 percent 24,181
Risk reversal credit Rs 7,378 Unlimited 60 percent 24,264

The three share a bearish direction but split sharply on risk. The put ratio back spread is the only one with a defined, modest loss, Rs 20,670 at worst, which makes it the most beginner-friendly of the three despite its exotic shape. Its price is a low 32 percent chance of profit, because it needs a real fall to pay. The short synthetic future and the risk reversal both open for a credit and show a higher probability of profit, but both carry the uncapped upside loss of a sold call, and both demand a large margin to hold.

Did you know

Look at the margins. The defined-risk put ratio back spread blocks Rs 46,304, while the two undefined-risk trades each block over Rs 1.6 lakh. The exchange is telling you, in cash, exactly how much more dangerous an uncapped position is. When a structure ties up three to four times the margin, that is your signal to check where the unlimited tail lives.

Tip

If you are bearish and still learning, prefer the put ratio back spread from this trio, or better still the bear put spread from the last chapter. Both keep your worst case printed on the ticket. Leave the short synthetic and the risk reversal until you fully understand and can manage an uncapped short, and never hold one through an event without a plan to cap the upside.

You can build all three in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), placing the legs and watching how the put ratio back spread carves its loss valley, how the synthetic draws a perfectly straight line, and how the risk reversal slopes between its strikes. Drag the modelled price upward on the two credit trades and watch the loss line fall without end. That sight is the lesson. Next we return to safe, defined-risk territory with the bearish butterfly and the bearish condor, two cheap and targeted ways to bet on a precise landing below the money.

12.html:
The bearish trades so far have all rewarded a fall, the bigger the better. This chapter teaches the opposite instinct. What if you do not just think NIFTY will drop, but you think it will drop to a particular place and stop, settling into a narrow band a little below where it sits now? When your view is that precise, you can buy a structure that costs almost nothing and pays many times its cost if the index lands exactly where you guessed. These are the bearish butterfly and the bearish condor, built entirely from puts, sitting just below the money. They are cheap, they are defined-risk, and they offer a dazzling reward against a tiny outlay. The honest catch, which this chapter will not hide, is that the odds of winning are low, because the price has to thread a needle.

The bearish butterfly

A butterfly has three strikes and four legs. You buy one put above, sell two in the middle, and buy one below, so the two bought wings fence in the two sold puts at the centre. Placed below the current spot, the whole structure peaks if NIFTY drifts down to the middle strike.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Put upper wing
2 Sell 23,950 Put (x2) body
3 Buy 23,850 Put lower wing

The two sold puts at the body bring in most of the premium, and the two bought wings cost a little more, leaving a tiny net debit of about Rs 393 for the whole lot. That debit is your maximum loss, suffered if NIFTY finishes outside the wings, above 24,050 or below 23,850, where the structure is worth nothing. The maximum profit is Rs 6,107, reached only if NIFTY lands precisely on the 23,950 body at expiry, where the two sold puts are worth the full wing width against worthless wings working for you.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 6,107
Max loss Rs 393
Breakevens 23,856 and 24,047
Probability of profit 9 percent
Risk to reward 1:16
Net debit Rs 393
Margin required Rs 63,606

The orange at-expiry line draws a sharp tent. It sits on the small loss of Rs 393 below 23,850 and above 24,050. Between the breakevens of 23,856 and 24,047 it pokes above zero, climbing to its peak right at the 23,950 body. The pink spot line at 24,056 sits just to the right of the whole tent, which tells the story plainly: NIFTY has to come down into the narrow profit band for this trade to pay, and finish near 23,950 to pay the most.

Key idea

A bearish butterfly buys a put above, sells two puts at the body, and buys a put below, all below the current spot. It costs a tiny debit, that debit is the most you can lose, and it pays many times over if NIFTY drifts down and pins the body strike at expiry. It is a targeted bet on a precise landing, not on a big move.

The bearish condor

The condor is a butterfly with its peak stretched into a plateau. Instead of selling two puts at one body strike, you sell them at two adjacent strikes, which spreads the maximum profit across a small range rather than a single point. That makes it a little more forgiving about exactly where NIFTY lands, at the cost of a lower peak.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Put upper wing
2 Sell 24,000 Put inner
3 Sell 23,900 Put inner
4 Buy 23,850 Put lower wing

The two sold inner puts again bring in most of the premium, leaving a net debit of just Rs 36 for the lot, the cheapest entry in this entire chapter. That Rs 36 is the maximum loss. The maximum profit is Rs 3,214, earned across the plateau if NIFTY finishes anywhere between the two inner sold strikes of 23,900 and 24,000 at expiry.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 3,214
Max loss Rs 36
Breakevens 23,837 and 24,055
Probability of profit 10 percent
Risk to reward 1:90
Net debit Rs 36
Margin required Rs 63,256

The orange line crosses zero at 23,837 and 24,055, marking the edges of the profit zone, and runs flat across the plateau between the inner strikes. Compared with the butterfly, the tent has become a tabletop: a lower ceiling of Rs 3,214 instead of Rs 6,107, but earned over a wider landing zone instead of a single point.

Did you know

Look at the reward to risk. The butterfly reads 1:16 and the condor an astonishing 1:90, because the Rs 36 debit is so small against the Rs 3,214 it can earn. These look like the best trades in the course. The number that keeps you honest is the probability of profit, just 9 to 10 percent. A 1:90 payoff that wins one time in ten is a lottery ticket, not an edge.

Why the odds are low, told honestly

The dazzling reward to risk and the tiny cost make these structures look almost too good. The catch is geometry. To win, NIFTY must finish inside a band only a couple of hundred points wide, and to win the most it must finish in an even narrower slice. With about 32 days to expiry and the sigma bands on the chart showing how far NIFTY could roam, the chance of it settling into that exact pocket below the money is genuinely small. That is what the 9 to 10 percent probability of profit is measuring, and it is not a flaw in the trade. It is the price of the huge payoff. The market does not hand out 1:90 odds on likely events.

So the cheapness is a feature and a trap at once. Because one lot risks only Rs 36 to Rs 393, it is tempting to scale up to many lots and treat the structure as nearly free. Do not. Stacking lots multiplies a low-probability bet, and the small per-lot loss becomes a real loss across size, suffered nine or ten times out of ten.

Heads up

A near-zero cost is not a near-zero risk once you size up. Ten lots of the bearish condor risk Rs 360 and ten lots of the butterfly risk Rs 3,930, and you will lose that full amount roughly nine times out of ten, because the probability of profit is only about 10 percent. Treat these as small, deliberate, low-odds bets, not as free money to load up on.

When these earn their place

Used with clear eyes, the bearish butterfly and condor have a real role. They are the right tool when you hold a specific, time-bound view that NIFTY will ease down to a particular level and stall there, perhaps into a known support or ahead of an event you expect to pin the index. You are paying a few hundred rupees for a defined-risk ticket on that precise outcome, and if you are right the payoff dwarfs the cost.

  • Choose the butterfly when your view of the landing spot is sharp and you want the highest payoff for pinning the 23,950 body exactly.
  • Choose the condor when you want a little more room, accepting a lower ceiling in exchange for a plateau that pays anywhere from 23,900 to 24,000.

Both keep your worst case printed on the ticket, Rs 393 or Rs 36, which is exactly the discipline this course keeps pressing. The margin, around Rs 63,000, reflects the four legs the exchange must track, but your actual cash at risk is only the tiny debit.

Tip

Place these closer to expiry, not far from it. A butterfly or condor pinned weeks early gives NIFTY too much time to wander out of the narrow band. The same structure put on in the final week, when the sigma bands have shrunk and you have a strong read on where the index will settle, has a far better chance of landing in the pocket.

Note

The estimated premium on these trades is a turnover figure across all four legs and looks large, but it is not your risk. Your risk is the small net debit, Rs 393 or Rs 36, because the bought wings cap every outcome. Never read the gross premium as the amount you can lose; read the net debit, which the builder reports as the maximum loss.

You can construct both in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), adding the legs one at a time and watching the sharp tent of the butterfly soften into the tabletop of the condor as you split the body into two strikes. Slide the modelled price across the chart and see how rarely it lands in the profit band, and how richly it pays on the few occasions it does. That feel, cheap and brilliant but seldom right, is exactly the honest lesson of the directional butterfly and condor. With this chapter the bearish family is complete, and next we turn to the trades that do not care about direction at all, betting instead on how far NIFTY moves.

toc.text: Why the odds are low, told honestly

13.html:
Every strategy so far has needed a direction. You leaned bullish or you leaned bearish, and you chose a structure to match. Now meet the first trades that do not care which way NIFTY goes, only how far. Picture the index sitting at 24,056 with a big event on the calendar, a policy decision or a result that everyone knows will land but nobody can call in advance. You are convinced NIFTY is about to move hard. You simply do not know whether up or down. The long straddle and the long strangle are built for exactly that moment. You buy both a call and a put, so a large move in either direction pays, and your only real enemy is a market that goes nowhere.

The long straddle: buying both sides at the money

A long straddle is the purest big-move bet there is. You buy the at-the-money call and the at-the-money put on the same strike and the same expiry, here the 24,050 strike expiring 28 July 2026. One leg profits if NIFTY climbs, the other if it falls, and you own both.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 paid
2 Buy 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 paid

Add the two premiums. The call costs about Rs 27,677 and the put about Rs 19,182, for a total net debit of Rs 46,858 for one lot of 65. That debit is large because you are buying two at-the-money options at once, each fat with time value. Because you paid it up front and can never be asked for more, that Rs 46,858 is also your maximum loss, suffered only if NIFTY finishes exactly on 24,050 at expiry, where both options expire worthless together. The margin the exchange blocks is simply the debit itself, Rs 46,858, because this is a fully defined-risk trade.

The maximum profit is unlimited. Above the upper breakeven the call runs without a ceiling, so the position keeps gaining the further NIFTY climbs. On a fall the put gains too, all the way down toward zero, a very large but technically finite amount. The builder reports the upside as unlimited, which is the honest label for a payoff that has no cap.

Key idea

A long straddle buys the at-the-money call and the at-the-money put together. It profits from a big move in either direction and loses only if NIFTY sits still. The premium you pay, here Rs 46,858, is the whole risk, and the reward is open ended on a genuine move.

The two breakevens are 23,329 and 24,771. Those come straight from the debit. You paid about 721 points a share in total premium, so NIFTY has to travel more than 721 points down to 23,329 or up to 24,771 just to cover what you spent. Only beyond those marks does the straddle turn a profit. The probability of profit is 42 percent, the market-implied chance that NIFTY finishes outside that band by 28 July. It sits below half because a 721-point move, roughly three percent of the index in about a month, is a real distance to ask for.

Reading the long straddle chart

The orange at-expiry line draws a clean V. Its lowest point sits right at the 24,050 strike, on the full loss of Rs 46,858, because that is the one place both options die worthless. Walk outward from there in either direction and the loss shrinks as one leg comes alive. At 23,329 on the left and 24,771 on the right the line crosses zero, the amber breakeven dots, and beyond them you climb into profit, steeper and steeper. The pink spot line at 24,056 stands almost on top of the trough, which says it plainly: you have entered at the worst point on the at-expiry curve and you need NIFTY to walk a long way out of the valley.

Now look at the blue dashed T+0 line, the position today. Near the centre it floats well above the orange V, because the two options are still stuffed with time value. That gap is the catch. As the days pass and nothing happens, the blue line sags down toward the orange, and your position loses money even when NIFTY barely moves. That sag is time decay, and for a straddle buyer it is a daily tax.

Heads up

A long straddle bleeds time value every calm day. You are long two at-the-money options at once, so the decay runs against you twice as fast. The Rs 46,858 you paid is at risk not only if NIFTY stays put but also if it drifts slowly, because the move has to be large and reasonably quick to outrun the decay. Never treat a straddle as a position to hold and forget. It is a bet that something happens soon.

The long strangle: the same idea, made cheaper

The straddle is powerful but expensive, because both legs sit at the money where time value is richest. The long strangle keeps the same shape for less money by buying out-of-the-money options instead. You buy the put two strikes below and the call two strikes above, here the 23,950 put and the 24,150 call.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 23,950 Put out-of-the-money
2 Buy 24,150 Call out-of-the-money

Because both legs are out of the money, each costs less, and the net debit falls to Rs 40,729 for the lot, about Rs 6,000 cheaper than the straddle. That smaller debit is the maximum loss, and the margin equals it. The trade-off is that the cheaper entry buys you a worse breakeven, not a better one.

The breakevens are 23,323 and 24,777, wider than the straddle on both sides. The strangle gives back nothing in the middle. Between 23,950 and 24,150 both options are worthless at expiry, so across that whole central band you simply lose the full Rs 40,729. The probability of profit is 41 percent, a touch lower than the straddle, because although you paid less, NIFTY now has to clear an even wider gap before either leg pays.

On the chart the V has become a bucket. Instead of a single sharp trough, the orange line runs flat along the bottom across the gap between the two strikes, then turns up only outside the wings. The breakevens sit a little farther out than the straddle's, at 23,323 and 24,777. You have paid less to put the trade on, and in return NIFTY must move a little more before you are paid.

Straddle against strangle, and the move you are buying

Put the two side by side and the choice becomes a simple bargain about cost versus distance.

Number Long Straddle Long Strangle
Max profit Unlimited Unlimited
Max loss Rs 46,858 Rs 40,729
Breakevens 23,329 and 24,771 23,323 and 24,777
Probability of profit 42 percent 41 percent
Net debit Rs 46,858 Rs 40,729
Margin required Rs 46,858 Rs 40,729

The straddle costs more but starts working a few points sooner, because its legs are already at the money. The strangle costs less but asks for a slightly bigger move before it pays. Neither is free, and both share the same Achilles heel: they need NIFTY to actually go somewhere.

Did you know

Look at what the market is charging you to bet on a move. The straddle's Rs 46,858 debit means NIFTY has to travel about 721 points just to reach breakeven, the strangle about 727 points. Those distances are the option market's own estimate of how far NIFTY might roam by 28 July, drawn on the chart as the sigma bands. You are not getting a cheap bet on volatility, you are paying the fair price for it.

Tip

The best time to buy a straddle or strangle is when implied volatility is low and you expect it to rise, not after it has already spiked ahead of a known event. If you buy into already expensive options, even a real move can disappoint, because the volatility you paid for drains out of both legs once the event passes. Buy the move before the crowd prices it in, not after.

Note

The one comfort in both trades is that the risk is fully defined and the margin equals the debit. You can lose Rs 46,858 on the straddle or Rs 40,729 on the strangle and not a rupee more, no matter how violently NIFTY swings. That is the gift of being a buyer. Your worst case is printed on the ticket before you ever place the order, which is exactly the discipline a beginner should keep.

You can build both in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), adding the call and the put one leg at a time and watching the single line of a long option fold into the V of a straddle, then spread into the bucket of a strangle as you push the strikes apart. Slide the modelled price across the chart and feel how rarely a calm day helps you and how richly a real move pays. That instinct, that you are buying motion and fighting the clock, is the heart of every long-volatility trade. In the next chapter we flip to the other side of these very structures, where you sell the straddle and the strangle to collect that rich premium, and inherit a risk that runs the other way.

toc.text: The long straddle: buying both sides at the money
The long strangle: the same idea, made cheaper
Straddle against strangle, and the move you are buying

14.html:
In the last chapter you were the buyer, paying a rich premium and praying for a big move. Now stand on the other side of that very trade. Someone has to sell the straddle buyer their call and their put, collect that fat premium, and bet the opposite way: that NIFTY goes nowhere. That seller is you in this chapter. The short straddle and the short strangle are the income trades of the calm market. You take in the premium up front, and every quiet day pulls the position toward profit. The shape is seductive and the win rate is high. The danger is that you have inherited the buyer's unlimited reward as your own unlimited loss, and that changes everything.

The short straddle: selling both sides at the money

A short straddle is the exact inverse of the long straddle. You sell the at-the-money call and the at-the-money put on the same 24,050 strike expiring 28 July 2026, and you collect both premiums at once.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 received
2 Sell 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 received

Add the two premiums and you take in a net credit of Rs 46,858 for one lot, money that lands in your account the moment you open the trade. That credit is your maximum profit, kept in full only if NIFTY finishes exactly on 24,050 at expiry, where both options you sold expire worthless and you owe nothing. The breakevens are 23,329 and 24,771, the same two marks as the long straddle, because you have simply taken the other side. NIFTY can wander anywhere inside that 1,442-point band and you still finish in the black. The probability of profit is 58 percent, comfortably above half, because most months NIFTY does not travel 721 points either way.

That high win rate is the seduction. Here is the truth the credit hides.

Heads up

A short straddle has a maximum profit of Rs 46,858 and a maximum loss that is UNLIMITED. You have sold a naked call, so a strong rally costs you rupee for rupee above 24,771 with no ceiling at all, and you have sold a naked put, so a crash costs you all the way down to 23,329 and far below. This is two uncovered short legs stacked on the same strike. The exchange blocks a margin of about Rs 1.62 lakh precisely because the risk is open ended. A single gap, up or down, can erase many months of quiet credits in one morning.

Reading the short straddle chart

The orange at-expiry line is the long straddle's V turned upside down. Its peak sits at the 24,050 strike, on the full credit of Rs 46,858, which is the most you can ever make. Walk outward in either direction and the profit shrinks, crossing zero at the breakevens of 23,329 and 24,771, the amber dots. Beyond those marks the line plunges into the red and keeps falling, with no floor on either side. The pink spot line at 24,056 sits almost on the peak, which is the appeal: you have entered at the very top of the at-expiry curve, and you only need NIFTY to stay near home.

The blue dashed T+0 line tells you how you get paid. Near the centre it sits below the orange peak, because the options you sold still hold the time value that has not yet decayed in your favour. Every calm day, that blue line lifts toward the orange peak as the time value you collected melts. Time decay, the enemy of the straddle buyer, is now your ally. The whole trade is a wager that decay arrives faster than any move that hurts you.

The short strangle: a wider safe zone

The short straddle pays the most but its breakevens are uncomfortably close to spot. The short strangle buys you a wider margin for error by selling out-of-the-money options instead. You sell the 23,950 put and the 24,150 call, leaving a cushion on each side before either leg comes alive.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Sell 23,950 Put out-of-the-money
2 Sell 24,150 Call out-of-the-money

Because both legs are out of the money, each fetches less premium, so the net credit falls to Rs 40,729 for the lot, about Rs 6,000 less than the straddle. In exchange you get a flat profit plateau in the middle rather than a single peak. The breakevens are 23,323 and 24,777, wider than the straddle's, and between 23,950 and 24,150 you keep the entire credit because both options expire worthless across that band. The probability of profit is 59 percent, a fraction higher than the straddle, because you have given NIFTY more room to roam before it touches you.

But the wider safe zone does not change the nature of the trade. The loss is still uncapped.

Heads up

A short strangle has a maximum profit of Rs 40,729 and a maximum loss that is still UNLIMITED. The wider breakevens make it feel safer, and the higher 59 percent probability of profit flatters it further, but both sold legs are naked. Above 24,777 the short call bleeds without limit, and below 23,323 the short put behaves like an obligation to buy NIFTY all the way down. The margin is about Rs 1.62 lakh for the same reason as the straddle. A higher win rate on a trade that can lose without bound is not safety, it is a slower fuse.

On the chart the inverted V has flattened into a tabletop. The orange line runs flat along the top across the gap between the two strikes, where the full Rs 40,729 is yours, then turns down outside the wings and falls away on both sides. The plateau is the comfort, the falling tails are the danger, and the tails never stop.

The two trades side by side, and the risk that defines them

Number Short Straddle Short Strangle
Max profit Rs 46,858 Rs 40,729
Max loss Unlimited Unlimited
Breakevens 23,329 and 24,771 23,323 and 24,777
Probability of profit 58 percent 59 percent
Net credit Rs 46,858 Rs 40,729
Margin required Rs 162,339 Rs 162,195

Read the columns and the bargain is clear. The straddle collects the larger credit but with a tighter safe zone. The strangle collects less but gives you more room to be wrong. Both win most of the time, and both share the one feature that should make a beginner pause: the loss has no limit and the margin is large. You are collecting a few thousand rupees of premium while putting more than a lakh and a half of capital on the line, exposed to a tail that can take far more than you ever took in.

Did you know

The high probability of profit is exactly why these trades are dangerous to the unwary. A 58 or 59 percent win rate means you will collect the credit again and again, building quiet confidence, and the rare loss that finally arrives can be many times larger than every gain before it. The numbers reward you on most days and punish you savagely on the few that matter. That is the opposite of the defined-risk discipline this course keeps pressing.

Heads up

If you ever do sell premium this way, never hold a naked short straddle or strangle without a plan to cap the loss. The professional fix is to buy a cheaper, further out-of-the-money call and put as insurance, which turns the unlimited tails into known numbers and converts the trade into an iron fly or an iron condor. Those are the defined-risk versions of this exact idea, and they are where the next two chapters go. A beginner should learn the short straddle and strangle mainly to respect them, not to place them naked.

Tip

The market-implied odds are computed for a single hold to expiry. Many sellers never reach expiry. They take the trade off once a large part of the credit has decayed, locking a partial profit and stepping out of the path of a late move, or they exit the moment NIFTY threatens a breakeven, accepting a small loss rather than waiting for an unlimited one. The exit plan matters more here than on any defined-risk trade in this course.

You can rehearse both structures in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), selling the call and the put and watching the inverted V of the straddle spread into the tabletop of the strangle as you push the strikes apart. Slide the modelled price toward a breakeven and watch the falling tail keep going, with no floor to catch it. Feel both halves of the trade at once: the quiet daily pull of decay in your favour, and the open cliff on either side that one violent session can send you over. That tension, steady income against an uncapped tail, is what every premium seller lives with. In the next chapter we fence those tails in, building the iron condor that keeps the income while putting a hard floor under the loss.

toc.text: The short straddle: selling both sides at the money
The short strangle: a wider safe zone
The two trades side by side, and the risk that defines them

15.html:
The short strangle from the last chapter had a lovely income shape and one fatal flaw: tails that fell forever. The obvious question is whether you can keep the steady premium while putting a hard floor under the loss. You can, and the structure that does it is the iron condor, the most popular defined-risk income trade in the options world. The idea is simple. Take the sold strangle and buy a cheaper, further out-of-the-money call and put as insurance. Those two bought wings turn the unlimited tails into known numbers. What you are left with is a four-leg structure that profits inside a range and can never lose more than a fixed, modest amount. NIFTY, the deepest index option market in the country, is the natural home for it.

From a naked strangle to a fenced range

An iron condor is two vertical spreads sharing one expiry: a bull put spread on the downside and a bear call spread on the upside, placed around the current price. Here is the version the OpenAlgo builder calls the Long Iron Condor, built tight to the money on the 28 July 2026 expiry.

Leg Action Strike Type Role
1 Buy 23,850 Put lower wing
2 Sell 23,950 Put inner short
3 Sell 24,150 Call inner short
4 Buy 24,250 Call upper wing

The two inner sold options, the 23,950 put and the 24,150 call, are the same legs as a short strangle and bring in most of the premium. The two outer bought options, the 23,850 put and the 24,250 call, cost a little and act as insurance, capping each tail one hundred points out. The net is a credit of Rs 5,616 that lands in your account when you open the trade. That credit is your maximum profit, kept in full if NIFTY finishes between the inner strikes of 23,950 and 24,150 at expiry, where all four legs settle in your favour.

Now the loss is fenced. Each wing is one hundred points wide, worth Rs 6,500 on a lot of 65, and you already collected Rs 5,616 of that, so the most you can lose is the gap, Rs 884. That is the whole difference between this and the naked strangle: the worst case has gone from unlimited to Rs 884, and the margin has fallen from over a lakh and a half to about Rs 64,319.

Key idea

An iron condor is a bull put spread plus a bear call spread sharing one expiry. The inner sold strikes collect the premium, and the outer bought wings cap both tails. It is the defined-risk version of the short strangle: the same range-bound income shape, but with a known, small maximum loss instead of an open cliff.

Reading the long iron condor chart

The orange at-expiry line draws a flat-topped table. Across the gap between the inner strikes it runs flat at the full credit of Rs 5,616, the green plateau, then steps down on each side as one spread comes alive, flattening again at the small Rs 884 loss once NIFTY passes a wing. The amber breakevens sit at 23,864 and 24,236, the points where the credit you banked is exactly eaten by a leg moving against you. The pink spot line at 24,056 sits inside the plateau, near its centre, so today you are sitting in the profitable zone.

But notice how narrow that profit band is. The breakevens are only about 190 points on each side of spot, and the sigma bands on the chart show NIFTY can easily travel three or four times that far by expiry. That is why the probability of profit is just 17 percent. The structure pays beautifully against its risk, a reward to risk of 1 to 6.4, because the inner strikes hug the money and collect a rich credit. The price of that rich credit is a tight, low-odds profit zone. You are most likely to walk away with the small Rs 884 loss, and only occasionally with the full Rs 5,616.

Did you know

This is the same honest tension you met with the directional butterflies. A reward to risk of 1 to 6.4 looks wonderful until you read the 17 percent probability of profit beside it. The market does not give away a six-to-one payoff on a likely event. The big credit and the tiny risk are paid for with a narrow band that NIFTY usually escapes.

The short iron condor: the high-probability inverse

The builder also offers the exact mirror of this trade, which it calls the Short Iron Condor. You take the other side of all four legs: buy the inner strikes, sell the outer wings, and pay a debit of Rs 5,616 instead of collecting it.

Leg Action Strike Type Role
1 Sell 23,850 Put lower wing
2 Buy 23,950 Put inner long
3 Buy 24,150 Call inner long
4 Sell 24,250 Call upper wing

Because it is the inverse, every number flips. The maximum profit is Rs 884, earned whenever NIFTY finishes outside the breakevens, that is, anywhere except pinned inside the narrow central band. The maximum loss is Rs 5,616, suffered only if NIFTY settles between the inner strikes at expiry. The breakevens are the same 23,864 and 24,236, and the margin is about Rs 68,864. The headline number is the probability of profit of 83 percent, because NIFTY almost always drifts at least a little away from dead centre over 32 days, and any such drift hands this version its small win.

So you have one structure and two faces. One collects a big credit and rarely keeps it. The other pays a debit, wins a little most of the time, and loses a lot on the rare occasion NIFTY sits perfectly still.

Which one for income, and the lesson of the labels

A trader chasing income wants to be right often, to collect a small, repeatable edge rather than swing for a jackpot. By that single test, the high-probability structure is the one to study, here the version the builder labels the Short Iron Condor at 83 percent. Being right four times in five feels like income. But the nine numbers tell you the rest of the story, and it is not all comfort.

  • The high-probability side wins only Rs 884 when it is right and loses Rs 5,616 when it is wrong. A handful of wins is undone by a single loss, so winning often is not the same as winning overall.
  • The big-credit side collects Rs 5,616 but is right only 17 percent of the time, so its rare wins must carry a long string of small Rs 884 losses.
  • Either way the position is defined-risk: your worst case is printed on the ticket, Rs 884 on one side or Rs 5,616 on the other, with no open tail. That alone makes the iron condor a far safer income tool than the naked strangle it is built from.
Heads up

Do not trust the words Long and Short on a builder, and do not assume a credit means a good trade or a debit a bad one. Here the Long Iron Condor is the credit version and the Short Iron Condor is the debit version, which runs against most people's intuition. The only honest way to judge any structure is to read its nine numbers: max profit, max loss, the breakevens, the probability of profit, and whether the credit or debit fits the outcome you actually expect. The label can mislead you. The numbers cannot.

Tip

Real income condors are usually placed wider than this tight example. Push the inner short strikes further out of the money and the profit band widens, the probability of profit climbs, and the credit shrinks. You trade a smaller reward for better odds and a calmer night. The builder lets you drag the strikes and watch the plateau stretch and the probability rise in real time, so you can find the balance of credit and odds that suits your view before risking a rupee.

Note

The margin on either version, about Rs 64,000 to Rs 69,000, reflects the four legs the exchange must track, but your true cash at risk is only the defined maximum loss, Rs 884 or Rs 5,616. That is the whole reason traders accept the lower reward of a condor over a naked strangle: a heavy, open-ended margin and an unlimited tail are replaced by a modest, knowable risk you can size with confidence.

Build both faces in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), adding the four legs and watching the plateau form, then flipping every leg to see the payoff turn upside down. Slide the modelled price across the band and feel how often NIFTY escapes the narrow zone, and how that single fact decides which version wins. The iron condor is the workhorse of defined-risk income, and reading its two faces correctly is one of the most useful skills in this whole course. In the next chapter we tighten the range to a single point, collapsing the condor's plateau into the sharp peak of the iron fly and the butterfly.

Reading the long iron condor chart
The short iron condor: the high-probability inverse
Which one for income, and the lesson of the labels

16.html:
The iron condor pays you to keep NIFTY inside a range. This chapter tightens that range until it is a single point. If your view is not just that NIFTY will stay calm but that it will settle almost exactly where it sits now, near 24,050, you can build a structure that peaks sharply at that one strike and falls away on both sides. These are the iron fly and the butterfly, the pin-the-strike trades. They cost almost nothing or collect a rich credit, they are fully defined-risk, and they pay many times their cost if NIFTY lands on the nail. The honest catch, the same one that shadowed the directional butterflies, is that pinning a single strike is a low-odds event. This chapter shows three different ways to build the very same peak, and is clear about the slim chance of hitting it.

The iron fly: a condor with its plateau collapsed

An iron fly is an iron condor whose two inner short strikes have been pulled together onto the same strike. Instead of selling a put and a call a little apart, you sell both at the money, then buy a wing on each side for protection. The OpenAlgo builder calls the credit version the Long Iron Fly.

Leg Action Strike Type Role
1 Buy 23,950 Put lower wing
2 Sell 24,050 Put body
3 Sell 24,050 Call body
4 Buy 24,150 Call upper wing

The two sold at-the-money options are a short straddle, and they collect a large premium, while the two bought wings cap each tail one hundred points out. The net is a credit of Rs 6,130, which is your maximum profit, kept in full only if NIFTY finishes exactly on 24,050 at expiry, where both sold options die together. Each wing is one hundred points wide, worth Rs 6,500 on a lot, and you collected Rs 6,130 of it, so the maximum loss is only Rs 370. The margin is about Rs 63,940. The breakevens are 23,956 and 24,148, a band barely 200 points wide, and the probability of profit is just 9 percent, because NIFTY has to settle almost dead centre. The reward to risk reads a dazzling 1 to 16.5.

The orange line is the condor's tabletop sharpened into a tent. It climbs to a single peak right at 24,050 and falls away steeply on both sides, flattening at the tiny Rs 370 loss past each wing. The pink spot line at 24,056 sits almost on the peak, which is the appeal and the trap at once. You are at the best point on the curve today, but the slightest drift in either direction walks you down the slope.

Key idea

An iron fly is a short straddle fenced by two bought wings. It is the iron condor with no plateau, peaking at a single strike. It collects a rich credit because the sold legs are at the money, caps the loss at a small known amount with the wings, and pays the most only if NIFTY pins the body strike at expiry.

The short iron fly: the high-probability inverse

As with the condor, the builder offers the mirror image, the Short Iron Fly. You take the other side of all four legs, paying a debit of Rs 6,130 instead of collecting it. Every number flips. The maximum profit is Rs 370, the maximum loss is Rs 6,130, and the probability of profit jumps to 91 percent, because NIFTY almost always drifts off the exact strike. The breakevens are the same 23,956 and 24,148, the margin about Rs 69,490, and the reward to risk a meagre 1 to 0.06.

So the iron fly, like the condor, has two faces. The credit Long Iron Fly is a long-shot bet on a precise pin, with a tiny risk and a rich reward at 9 percent odds. The debit Short Iron Fly wins a small amount almost every time NIFTY moves at all, and loses the large Rs 6,130 only on the rare day it freezes on the strike. Same structure, opposite wager.

Heads up

The same caution from the iron condor applies here, and harder. The 91 percent probability of profit on the Short Iron Fly is seductive, but it wins only Rs 370 against a Rs 6,130 loss. One bad pin undoes sixteen wins. And do not be misled by the Long and Short labels: the Long Iron Fly is the credit version, the Short Iron Fly the debit version, which runs against intuition. Judge each by its nine numbers, never by its name.

The butterfly: the same peak from one option type

Here is the elegant part. You can build that identical sharp tent without selling any straddle at all, using a single option type. A butterfly uses three strikes and four legs of one kind, calls or puts, to peak at the middle strike. The Call Butterfly does it with calls.

Leg Action Strike Type Role
1 Buy 23,950 Call lower wing
2 Sell 24,050 Call (x2) body
3 Buy 24,150 Call upper wing

You buy one call below, sell two at the body, and buy one above. The two sold calls bring in most of the premium and the two bought wings cost a little more, leaving a tiny net debit of Rs 348 for the whole lot. That debit is your maximum loss, suffered if NIFTY finishes outside the wings where the whole structure is worth nothing. The maximum profit is Rs 6,152, reached only if NIFTY lands precisely on the 24,050 body, where the lower wing is worth its full hundred points against worthless sold calls and an idle upper wing. The breakevens are 23,954 and 24,148, the probability of profit is 9 percent, and the reward to risk is a brilliant 1 to 17.7, on a margin of about Rs 64,000.

Lay this chart beside the long iron fly and they are almost the same picture: a sharp peak at 24,050, breakevens around 23,955 and 24,148, a huge reward against a tiny risk, and the same 9 percent odds. One was built from a credit straddle and two wings, the other from four calls for a small debit. Different legs, different cash flow, the same payoff.

Two routes to one shape

The Put Butterfly reaches the identical peak using only puts, the mirror construction of the call version.

Leg Action Strike Type Role
1 Buy 24,150 Put upper wing
2 Sell 24,050 Put (x2) body
3 Buy 23,950 Put lower wing

It costs a net debit of Rs 361, its maximum loss, and pays a maximum profit of Rs 6,139 if NIFTY pins 24,050. The breakevens are 23,955 and 24,148, the probability of profit again 9 percent, the reward to risk 1 to 17, and the margin about Rs 63,877. Place the call butterfly and the put butterfly side by side and you cannot tell their payoffs apart.

Did you know

The call butterfly costs Rs 348 and the put butterfly Rs 361, a few rupees apart, and both pin the same strike for the same reward and the same 9 percent odds. The tiny difference is just where each option sits relative to the money on the day you build it. The lesson is deeper than the numbers: a payoff shape is not tied to one option type. Calls and puts are two languages that describe the very same picture, and a skilled trader picks whichever legs are cheaper or more liquid to reach the shape they want.

Choosing among the pin-the-strike family

You now have three ways to build the same tent over 24,050: the credit iron fly, the call butterfly, and the put butterfly. They share a profit peak of roughly Rs 6,100 to Rs 6,150, a maximum loss of only a few hundred rupees, breakevens about 200 points wide, and a 9 percent probability of profit. The choice between them comes down to cash flow and liquidity, not outcome.

  • Choose a butterfly when you want to pay a tiny known debit, Rs 348 or Rs 361, and you would rather work in a single option type.
  • Choose the credit iron fly when you prefer to collect the premium up front and accept the same defined risk through the wings.
  • Use the short versions only with clear eyes: a high probability of a small win, paired with a large loss on the rare pin, is the opposite trade and demands tight management.
Tip

Place pin-the-strike trades close to expiry, not far from it. A fly or butterfly built weeks early gives NIFTY too much room to wander off the body strike. The same structure put on in the final days, when the sigma bands have shrunk and you have a strong read on where the index will settle, has a far better chance of landing on the nail. The peak is dazzling, but only if you collect it.

Note

As ever, the estimated premium across all four legs looks large, but it is not your risk. Your risk is the small net debit on a butterfly, Rs 348 or Rs 361, or the capped Rs 370 on the credit iron fly, because the bought wings fence every outcome. Read the maximum loss the builder reports, never the gross turnover.

Build all three in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo) and watch the same tent appear from a straddle plus wings, from four calls, and from four puts. Drag the modelled price a hundred points off the body and see how fast the peak collapses, a vivid lesson in how rarely a single strike is pinned and how richly it pays when it is. With the fly and butterfly the income and pin-the-strike families are complete, and from here the course turns to the ratio trades, where the legs are no longer balanced and a naked tail reappears.

The butterfly: the same peak from one option type

17.html:
The ratio back spreads from chapters six and eleven bought more options than they sold, so they opened for a debit, kept a defined loss, and paid off only on a big, fast move. This chapter flips that ratio on its head. Now you sell more than you buy. You collect a fat credit upfront, you profit most if NIFTY drifts gently toward the strike you sold, and you carry a naked tail that can run away if NIFTY moves too far in the wrong direction. These are the call ratio spread and the put ratio spread. They look clever and they often win, but the uncapped side is real, so read both charts with your eyes open.

The call ratio spread

A call ratio spread buys one at-the-money call and sells two calls a little higher. Because you sold one more call than you bought, the structure leans net short of calls above the higher strike, which is where the danger lives. You build it on the 28 July 2026 expiry like this.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Call about Rs 27,677 paid
2 Sell 24,150 Call (x2) about Rs 48,106 received

You pay about Rs 27,677 for the one bought call and take in about Rs 48,106 for the two sold calls, so the position opens for a healthy net credit of about Rs 20,430. That credit is yours to keep if NIFTY simply stays below 24,050, where every call expires worthless.

The clever part is the peak. As NIFTY rises from 24,050 toward 24,150, your bought call gains value while the two sold calls are still worthless, so your profit climbs. At exactly 24,150 the bought call is worth a full 100 points, about Rs 6,500, on top of the credit you already hold. That is the peak profit of Rs 26,930, reached when NIFTY pins the strike you sold.

Push past 24,150 and the two sold calls wake up against you. You are now short one net call, losing 65 rupees a point, so the profit melts away and then turns to loss. The breakeven is 24,564, the point where the at-expiry line crosses back through zero. Above it, the loss grows without any ceiling.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 26,930 (at 24,150)
Max loss Unlimited
Breakeven 24,564
Probability of profit 72 percent
Risk to reward NA
Net credit Rs 20,430
Margin required Rs 176,491
Heads up

A call ratio spread has a maximum loss that is UNLIMITED. The extra sold 24,150 call is naked, so once NIFTY climbs past the breakeven of 24,564 you lose 65 rupees for every point it keeps rising, with no ceiling at all. The exchange blocks about Rs 1.76 lakh of margin precisely because of that uncapped tail. The comfortable 72 percent probability of profit comes from the wide cushion before the loss starts, but a strong rally past the peak can turn a string of wins into one large loss.

Key idea

A ratio spread is a credit trade that wants NIFTY to land near the strike you sold. It profits across a wide zone, peaks exactly at the sold strike, and then exposes a naked tail beyond it. The credit you collect is the reward for carrying that uncapped risk.

Reading the call ratio spread chart

The chart is the real payoff, drawn from live NIFTY data captured on 26 June 2026, with spot 24,056, the 24,050 and 24,150 strikes, lot 65, and about 32 days to expiry. Walk the orange at-expiry line from left to right. Below 24,050 it sits flat on the credit of Rs 20,430, the green floor. From 24,050 it ramps up to the peak of Rs 26,930 at 24,150, then slopes down, crosses zero at the breakeven of 24,564, and plunges into the red beyond it with no floor.

The blue dashed T+0 (today) line is smoother and sits below the peak, because the sold time value has not yet decayed. Every calm day pulls the position up toward that orange peak. The faint sigma bands show the move the option market is pricing in by expiry, and the pink line marks spot at 24,056, just left of the rising ramp.

Note

The estimated premium here is about Rs 75,783, the gross value of all three legs added together. That is a turnover figure, not your risk. Your total P&L sits at about zero the moment you enter, because you have just exchanged fair value. Both numbers only move as NIFTY does.

The put ratio spread

The put ratio spread is the same idea pointed downward. You buy one at-the-money put and sell two puts a little lower, leaving you net short one put below the lower strike.

Leg Action Strike Type Premium per lot
1 Buy 24,050 Put about Rs 19,182 paid
2 Sell 23,950 Put (x2) about Rs 33,352 received

You pay about Rs 19,182 for the one bought put and collect about Rs 33,352 for the two sold puts, opening for a net credit of about Rs 14,170. You keep that credit in full if NIFTY stays above 24,050.

The peak sits at the lower strike. As NIFTY falls from 24,050 toward 23,950, the bought put gains while the sold puts stay worthless, lifting profit to its maximum of Rs 20,670 when NIFTY pins 23,950, where the bought put is worth a full 100 points on top of the credit. Below 23,950 the two sold puts turn against you. You are now short one net put, losing 65 rupees a point as NIFTY keeps falling. The breakeven is 23,632, and below it the loss grows toward a very large figure on a genuine collapse.

Number Value
Max profit Rs 20,670 (at 23,950)
Max loss Rs 1,536,080
Breakeven 23,632
Probability of profit 68 percent
Risk to reward 1:0.01
Net credit Rs 14,170
Margin required Rs 170,795
Heads up

A put ratio spread carries a very large downside loss, listed as Rs 1,536,080 if NIFTY fell toward zero. The extra sold 23,950 put is naked to the downside, so once NIFTY breaks below the breakeven of 23,632 you lose 65 rupees a point with only the floor of zero stopping it. That is why the exchange blocks about Rs 1.71 lakh of margin. The 68 percent probability of profit reflects the wide cushion above, not the depth of the tail. A sharp fall is exactly what hurts this trade most.

How a ratio spread differs from a back spread

It is easy to confuse these credit ratio spreads with the debit ratio back spreads from earlier. They are near opposites, and the difference is which side you over-weight.

  • A back spread buys two and sells one, so it is net long. It pays a small debit, keeps a defined loss, and needs a big, fast move to pay off.
  • A ratio spread sells two and buys one, so it is net short. It collects a credit, peaks if NIFTY pins the sold strike, and exposes a naked tail beyond it.

The back spread wants violence and accepts a low win rate for a huge payoff. The ratio spread wants a mild, steady drift toward the strike you sold and accepts a naked tail for a high win rate. Same legs, opposite ratio, opposite character.

Did you know

Look at the margins. Both ratio spreads block well over Rs 1.7 lakh, while the back spreads from chapters six and eleven blocked only about Rs 46,000 to Rs 52,000. The exchange is telling you in cash that the naked tail of a ratio spread is the more dangerous structure, even though it opens for a credit that feels like free money.

Tip

A ratio spread is a high-probability credit trade with a low-probability disaster attached. If you are still learning, prefer the defined-risk relatives: a bull call spread, a bear put spread, or the jade lizard you meet next, which keeps the credit while erasing one of the tails. Never hold a ratio spread through an event without a plan to cap the naked side, and size it so the worst case is survivable.

You can build both of these in sandbox trading (analyzer mode in OpenAlgo), placing the one long and two short legs and watching the orange line ramp up to its peak at the sold strike. Then drag the modelled price out past the breakeven and watch the at-expiry line fall away with no floor. That sight is the whole lesson of a naked tail. In the next chapter we take this credit idea and make it safer, using a single bought wing to erase one tail completely in the jade lizard and the reverse jade lizard.
